proposals.title: Adversarial Inverse Optimal Control for Scalable, Data-Driven Robotic Skill Acquisition
proposals.motivation: Inverse Optimal Control (IOC) is crucial for learning reward functions from expert demonstrations, enabling robots to acquire complex skills. However, traditional IOC methods often struggle with scalability and require hand-crafted features. Framing IOC as a distribution matching problem via Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) offers a promising, flexible alternative that can leverage large, diverse offline datasets like those in D5RL.
proposals.hypothesis: By formulating IOC as an adversarial imitation learning task, we can learn more expressive and scalable reward functions directly from high-dimensional observations, leading to improved policy performance in offline and sample-efficient online robotic reinforcement learning settings.
proposals.method: We will develop an adversarial IOC framework where a generator (a policy) tries to match the state-action distribution of an offline demonstration dataset, while a discriminator learns to distinguish between generated and expert trajectories. The discriminator's output will be used as a reward signal for training policies via offline RL algorithms, specifically building upon the Q-Transformer architecture for its scalability. We will integrate this into the SERL software suite to evaluate sample-efficient fine-tuning on real robotic tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, scalable adversarial IOC algorithm that seamlessly integrates with modern offline RL methods. It will demonstrate empirically that adversarial reward learning can improve policy performance on diverse robotic manipulation benchmarks from pre-existing datasets, advancing data-driven robotic skill acquisition.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial imitation learning (e.g., GAIL) is established for direct policy learning, its application specifically for learning generalizable, disentangled reward functions within an IOC framework—and its subsequent integration with powerful offline RL backbones like Q-Transformer—remains underexplored for robotics.

proposals.title: VIPER: Variational Inference for Policy Search with Entropic Regularization in Offline Robotic Learning
proposals.motivation: Offline reinforcement learning (RL) for robotics, as seen in D5RL and Q-Transformer, faces a key challenge: effectively leveraging diverse, suboptimal datasets while managing the uncertainty inherent in out-of-distribution actions. Current methods often rely on point estimates or constrained policy updates, which can be overly conservative or fail to capture the multi-modal nature of viable solutions in complex, real-world tasks. A principled Bayesian approach to policy search could better quantify uncertainty and improve robustness.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that framing offline policy search as variational inference—approximating a posterior distribution over policies conditioned on the offline dataset and a cost function—will yield more robust and sample-efficient policies for robotic manipulation by explicitly balancing task performance with distributional constraints.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework where the policy is a parameterized distribution, and we optimize a variational lower bound (ELBO) that trades off expected cost (negative reward) against the KL divergence from a prior policy, which can be learned from the offline data (e.g., via behavioral cloning). This builds upon pre-2026 variational RL and control-as-inference literature. We will integrate this with scalable architectures like the Q-Transformer for value estimation and test it within the SERL software suite on D5RL benchmark tasks and real robotic skill learning.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new, theoretically grounded algorithm for offline RL that unifies probabilistic inference with scalable value-based methods. It will provide empirical validation on robotic tasks, demonstrating improved robustness and exploration of solution modes compared to deterministic or constrained baseline methods. The released code will extend the SERL suite with new variational tools.
proposals.novelty: While variational methods exist in RL, their integration into modern, scalable offline RL architectures (like Transformer-based Q-learning) for robotic policy search is novel. The key insight is treating the entire policy optimization as an inference problem within these high-capacity, data-driven frameworks, moving beyond their typical use for simple exploration or posterior sampling.

proposals.title: One-Shot Imitation via Demonstration-Conditioned Offline Reinforcement Learning
proposals.motivation: Current offline RL and imitation learning methods require large, diverse datasets, which are expensive to collect for real-world robotic tasks. Enabling robots to learn from a single human demonstration would dramatically lower the barrier to programming robots for novel, long-horizon tasks. This addresses a core challenge in data-efficient robotic learning, aligning with the goal of generalist robots that can quickly adapt from minimal human input.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by conditioning a large, pre-trained, multi-task offline RL policy (like a Q-Transformer) on a single demonstration embedding, the policy can effectively generalize and perform the demonstrated task without further environment interaction. The key is robust demonstration representation and policy conditioning that prevents overfitting to the single example.
proposals.method: We will extend the Q-Transformer architecture to be demonstration-conditioned. A demonstration encoder (e.g., a small transformer) will process a single trajectory of state-action pairs into a task embedding. This embedding will be prepended to the history context input of the Q-Transformer, which was pre-trained on a diverse offline dataset (e.g., D5RL). We will fine-tune the combined model using a modified offline RL objective (e.g., Conservative Q-Learning) on the pre-training data, but with the demonstration encoder trained to maximize the Q-values for states and actions aligned with the provided demonstration. Evaluation will be conducted in simulated and real-world robotic manipulation benchmarks from SERL.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel method for one-shot visual imitation that leverages the robustness and scalability of offline RL. It will demonstrate how large, diverse static datasets can be harnessed to amplify the signal from a single demonstration. We will release benchmarks and models for one-shot robotic imitation, advancing the field towards more sample-efficient and flexible robot learning.
proposals.novelty: While prior work combines imitation with offline RL, our approach uniquely targets the extreme data-scarce regime of a single demonstration by using the demonstration as a conditioning signal for a generalist pre-trained policy, rather than for behavioral cloning or reward shaping. This differs from meta-learning or in-context learning approaches by leveraging the structured generalization of sequence-modeling Q-learning.

proposals.title: Compositional Offline RL: Modular Skill Policies from Heterogeneous Datasets
proposals.motivation: Current offline RL and multi-task learning methods predominantly rely on a single, shared network to learn a unified policy from diverse datasets (e.g., D5RL). This monolithic approach can lead to negative interference, catastrophic forgetting, and difficulty in adapting to new tasks by composing existing knowledge. A modular paradigm is needed to enable robust composition and efficient reuse of learned skills in real-world robotic applications.
proposals.hypothesis: A compositional policy architecture, where distinct skills are learned in separate modules and dynamically composed, will outperform monolithic multi-task policies in offline RL settings on metrics of zero-shot generalization, data efficiency for new tasks, and robustness to dataset heterogeneity.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework building on the Q-Transformer architecture, where each skill is represented by a separate, autoregressive Q-function module. A gating network, trained via an information-theoretic objective, will learn to select and combine these modules based on task context. We will leverage large, diverse offline datasets (D5RL-style) for pre-training and evaluate in the SERL software suite for sample-efficient fine-tuning and real-robot validation on novel task sequences.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new paradigm for offline multi-task RL that prioritizes compositionality over shared representations. It will yield a publicly available library of modular, reusable skill policies and a benchmark for compositional generalization in robotics, directly advancing towards more flexible and scalable robot learning systems.
proposals.novelty: While modular neural networks exist, their application to *offline* RL—where exploration is absent and data is fixed—presents a distinct challenge. This work inverts the core assumption of shared parameters in multi-task offline RL, explicitly designing for composition from static, heterogeneous data.

proposals.title: Robust Offline RL for Task-Generalization via Latent Skill Composition and Test-Time Adaptation
proposals.motivation: Current offline RL benchmarks and methods, including those in D5RL and SERL, largely assume training and evaluation tasks are drawn from the same distribution. This limits real-world applicability where robots must handle novel objects, unseen environments, or unforeseen goal specifications. A method that relaxes this assumption is crucial for robust, generalizable robotic learning from diverse, static datasets.
proposals.hypothesis: An offline RL agent trained on a diverse dataset of skills can, through test-time latent skill composition and adaptation, solve held-out tasks that are semantically related but distributionally shifted from its training tasks, without requiring online fine-tuning.
proposals.method: We will extend the Q-Transformer architecture to learn a discrete latent skill space from diverse offline data (e.g., D5RL, Robomimic). At test time, for a novel task specified via language or goal image, we will employ a lightweight, gradient-based adaptation loop that optimizes a skill-composition policy within this fixed latent space, using the offline-learned Q-function as a reward signal. This avoids online environment interaction and leverages the generalization of the pre-trained autoregressive value function.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a new benchmark for evaluating offline RL under task distribution shift, 2) a practical algorithm for zero-shot task generalization from offline data, and 3) empirical analysis of when latent skill composition fails, informing future work on representation learning for robotics.
proposals.novelty: While prior work like Q-Transformer excels at multi-task offline learning, it assumes test tasks are known during training. Our key novelty is the closed-loop, test-time adaptation mechanism within a fixed, pre-trained skill and value function space, enabling generalization to unseen task distributions without online RL.

proposals.title: SparseRewardMetaRL: Meta-Learning for Sample-Efficient Adaptation in Sparse-Reward Robotic Manipulation
proposals.motivation: Current offline and offline-to-online RL benchmarks for robotic manipulation, such as those in D5RL and Adroit, rely heavily on shaped, dense reward functions. This creates a significant sim-to-real gap, as real-world tasks often provide only sparse success signals. While meta-reinforcement learning (meta-RL) promises fast adaptation to new tasks, its efficacy in challenging, sparse-reward robotic settings remains underexplored, limiting its practical utility.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a meta-RL agent, trained on a diverse offline dataset of prior manipulation experiences with sparse rewards, can learn exploration strategies and temporal abstractions that enable sample-efficient online adaptation to novel sparse-reward tasks, outperforming methods reliant on shaped rewards.
proposals.method: We will leverage the Q-Transformer architecture as a base due to its success in offline RL with large, diverse datasets. We will extend it with a context-based meta-learning framework, where a transformer encoder processes a context set of sparse-reward trajectories from the target task. This context will condition the Q-Transformer's policy and value functions for rapid online fine-tuning. The agent will be meta-trained on a new, procedurally generated sparse-reward benchmark built in the SERL software suite, learning to adapt from minimal interaction.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce: 1) a rigorous benchmark for evaluating meta-RL in sparse-reward robotic manipulation, 2) a novel meta-RL algorithm combining the strengths of offline RL (Q-Transformer) and context-based adaptation, and 3) empirical evidence demonstrating that dense reward shaping is not a prerequisite for efficient meta-learning in complex domains.
proposals.novelty: This directly challenges the prevailing assumption in meta-RL and offline RL benchmarks that shaped rewards are necessary for learning complex skills, proposing instead to meta-learn effective exploration and credit assignment purely from sparse signals. The integration of a high-capacity offline RL method (Q-Transformer) with meta-learning for sparse rewards is a novel combination.

proposals.title: Scaling Multi-Task Offline RL: Architectural and Algorithmic Bottlenecks at the Hundred-Task Regime
proposals.motivation: Current multi-task offline RL research, including work with D5RL and Q-Transformer, is typically validated on a few dozen tasks. Real-world robotic deployment requires systems that can master hundreds of diverse skills from large, static datasets. It is unknown whether existing architectures and algorithms will scale effectively or encounter new failure modes in this high-cardinality regime, limiting their practical applicability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that scaling to hundreds of tasks will expose critical bottlenecks in model capacity and credit assignment not seen at smaller scales, and that a modular architecture with shared skill representations and a scalable, autoregressive policy will outperform monolithic models.
proposals.method: We will extend the Q-Transformer architecture, which formulates Q-learning as sequence modeling, to a multi-task setting with hundreds of tasks. We will investigate a modular transformer with task-conditioned adapters and a shared foundation model, trained on a newly curated 'D5RL-100+' dataset spanning diverse robotic manipulation domains. We will rigorously benchmark this against monolithic Q-Transformers and other multi-task offline RL baselines, analyzing scaling laws, catastrophic interference, and positive transfer.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic study of offline RL at the hundred-task scale, identifying key scaling challenges and solutions. It will deliver a scalable, open-source multi-task Q-Transformer implementation and a large, public benchmark dataset to guide future research towards truly generalist robotic agents.
proposals.novelty: While multi-task RL is well-studied, the specific investigation of scaling laws and architectural bottlenecks for *offline* RL with hundreds of distinct tasks is unexplored. The approach uniquely combines the data-efficient, autoregressive structure of Q-Transformer with modular neural design to address this new regime.

proposals.title: Task-Centric Pruning: Identifying Minimal Task Sets for Robust Offline Robotic Generalization
proposals.motivation: Large, diverse datasets like D5RL have enabled offline RL for robotics, but collecting such data is expensive and often impractical. Current methods lack a principled understanding of which tasks are essential for learning generalizable skills, leading to inefficient use of data and compute. Determining the minimal effective task set would drastically reduce the cost of deploying data-driven robotic policies in new environments.
proposals.hypothesis: For a given target domain of robotic manipulation tasks, a small, strategically selected subset of training tasks—characterized by specific diversity metrics—can yield generalization performance comparable to training on a large, uncurated task set.
proposals.method: We will leverage offline datasets (e.g., D5RL) and the SERL framework to train Q-Transformer-style agents on progressively smaller, algorithmically selected task subsets. We will develop a task embedding space based on dynamics, success states, and visual features, then apply submodular optimization and counterfactual analysis to identify a minimal 'covering set' of tasks. Generalization will be evaluated on held-out tasks in simulation and on a real robot using the SERL suite.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal framework for quantifying the 'task efficiency' of offline robotic learning. It will yield practical algorithms for curating cost-effective robotic datasets and offer new theoretical insights into the relationship between task diversity and generalization in high-dimensional control problems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on scaling up data, we propose a principled approach to scale down, identifying the core tasks necessary for generalization. This inverts the standard paradigm from 'more data is better' to 'what is the minimal sufficient data?' for robotic skill acquisition.

proposals.title: Inverse Policy Inference: Recovering Task Intent from Multi-Task Robotic Behavior
proposals.motivation: Recent advances in offline RL and multi-task learning, such as Q-Transformer, have enabled the training of single, capable policies across diverse task families. However, once deployed, such a policy becomes a 'black box'—its internal task-specific intent is opaque. This limits interpretability, safety verification, and the ability to provide targeted human feedback or assistance. Inferring the underlying task from observed behavior is a crucial inverse problem for understanding and collaborating with learned agents.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the latent task structure embedded within a multi-task policy's behavior can be recovered by training a lightweight inference network on trajectories generated by that policy, and that this inferred task label can be used to improve policy fine-tuning and human-agent communication.
proposals.method: We will leverage pre-trained, frozen multi-task policies (e.g., Q-Transformer models) on datasets like D5RL to generate diverse robotic behavior trajectories. We will then train a temporal convolutional or transformer-based inference network to map observation-action sequences to a task embedding or discrete task ID. The approach will be evaluated on its classification accuracy, robustness to partial trajectories, and its utility for downstream applications such as conditioning a critic for fine-tuning or generating natural language explanations by aligning inferred tasks with dataset annotations.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a formal framework and benchmark for the inverse task inference problem in offline RL. It will provide a practical tool for diagnosing and interpreting the behavior of complex multi-task policies, bridging the gap between policy capability and human understanding. The resulting inference modules could become a standard component for deployable, transparent robotic systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on learning policies from task descriptions, we systematically study the inverse: recovering the task description from policy behavior. This shifts the focus from policy training to post-hoc analysis and transparency, a relatively unexplored direction in offline multi-task RL.

proposals.title: Trajectory-DPO: Aligning Dialogue Agents via Inverse Reinforcement Learning for Long-Horizon Preference Learning
proposals.motivation: Current alignment methods like Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) operate on single-turn responses, failing to capture the long-term coherence and strategic planning required for effective multi-turn dialogue. This is analogous to the credit assignment problem in robotics, where Inverse Reinforcement Learning (IRL) infers rewards from full trajectories. Adapting IRL principles can address the core challenge of aligning language model behavior across entire conversational histories.
proposals.hypothesis: A trajectory-level DPO variant, formulated by drawing a formal analogy to maximum-entropy Inverse Reinforcement Learning, can infer latent user preferences over multi-turn interactions and train policies that outperform single-turn DPO on metrics of long-term coherence and user satisfaction.
proposals.method: We will formalize a preferred dialogue history as a demonstrated state-action trajectory. Building on the maximum-entropy IRL framework and the DPO derivation, we will derive a trajectory-level objective that maximizes the likelihood of a full preferred conversation over a dispreferred one, using the autoregressive structure of language models. The implementation will extend the Q-Transformer's scalar reward modeling to sequence-level preference modeling, and evaluation will use diverse, multi-turn dialogue datasets with trajectory-wise preferences.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled method for credit assignment in conversational alignment, enabling the training of dialogue agents that optimize for long-term interaction goals. It will bridge techniques from robotic IRL and language model alignment, creating a new benchmark for evaluating multi-turn coherence.
proposals.novelty: While DPO and RLHF are applied per-turn, this is the first work to explicitly adapt the IRL formulation—specifically its trajectory-level credit assignment—to the language domain for direct policy optimization from full-conversation preferences.

proposals.title: Contrastive Online Preference Optimization: Adaptive Hard Negative Mining for Efficient Policy Improvement
proposals.motivation: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) has emerged as a powerful method for aligning models with human preferences offline, but its application to online, iterative settings like RLHF or robotic learning remains inefficient. Current online DPO approaches treat all new preference pairs equally, wasting updates on easy comparisons and failing to adapt to the evolving policy and data distribution. This limits the speed and robustness of learning from sequential feedback, a critical bottleneck for real-world deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating contrastive learning with adaptive hard negative mining into an online DPO framework will dynamically improve the quality of training comparisons, leading to more sample-efficient and robust policy improvement in iterative preference-based learning.
proposals.method: We will develop an online DPO agent that maintains a buffer of past policy outputs and newly sampled responses. For each new preferred response, we will use a noise-contrastive estimation loss to score candidate dispreferred responses from the buffer, selecting the hardest negatives (e.g., those with the smallest estimated reward margin). The policy will then be updated using a modified DPO loss on these adaptively mined pairs. We will integrate this into a robotic RL loop, building on the SERL infrastructure, to test it for tasks where preferences come from a learned reward model or human-in-the-loop.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a new algorithm for sample-efficient online preference optimization, demonstrated in simulated robotic control and possibly language tasks. It will provide a principled framework for treating negative example selection as a learnable, adaptive component within iterative alignment, moving beyond static datasets.
proposals.novelty: While contrastive learning and hard negative mining are established in representation learning, their integration into the online DPO loop for dynamic training data curation is novel. This bridges a gap between offline preference learning and adaptive online policy improvement methods.

proposals.title: Bayesian Nonparametric Preference Learning for Robust Offline Robotic Policy Alignment
proposals.motivation: Preference-based RL (PbRL) is a promising path for aligning robot policies with human intent, but current methods rely on the restrictive Bradley-Terry model, which can fail with inconsistent or sparse feedback common in real-world deployment. This mismatch leads to overconfident policy optimization and poor alignment. A more robust, data-driven approach is needed to handle the ambiguity inherent in human preferences, especially when learning from static offline datasets like those in D5RL.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling the preference probability function with a flexible Bayesian nonparametric prior, such as a Gaussian process, an agent can better capture epistemic uncertainty about the true reward model, leading to more robust policy optimization from offline data and more sample-efficient active querying.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework that places a Gaussian process prior over the latent preference function, integrating it with offline RL backbones like Q-Transformer. Inference will use sparse variational approximations for scalability. The learned uncertainty will guide conservative policy updates via pessimism for offline learning and inform an active query strategy based on Bayesian experimental design to resolve model ambiguity with minimal human comparisons.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled method for robust preference alignment from offline datasets, reducing sensitivity to feedback noise and model misspecification. It will yield new algorithmic tools for uncertainty-aware PbRL and demonstrate improved alignment performance on real robotic tasks from the SERL suite.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses Bayesian methods for uncertainty within a fixed preference model, this proposal introduces uncertainty over the model structure itself. This nonparametric approach to the core preference mapping is a significant departure from the standard Bradley-Terry assumption in PbRL.

proposals.title: Guarded Preference Meta-Learning: Robust and Safe Adaptation to Novel Human Objectives
proposals.motivation: Real-world robotic systems must adapt to diverse and evolving human preferences, often from limited feedback. Current meta-reinforcement learning and offline RL methods for preference alignment, like those building on Finn's prior work, can be brittle when preferences shift significantly or contradict prior data, risking unsafe or unstable behavior. A method that ensures robust and cautious adaptation is crucial for deploying adaptable agents in human-centric environments.
proposals.hypothesis: Meta-training a policy with a curriculum of simulated adversarial preference shifts, coupled with a guardrail module that screens fast-adaptation updates, will yield a system that adapts more robustly and safely to novel human preferences than standard meta-learning or offline fine-tuning approaches.
proposals.method: We will extend MAML-style meta-learning, where the outer loop trains on a diverse offline dataset (e.g., D5RL-style) augmented with a generative model of simulated preference functions, including contradictory rewards and complex trade-offs. The inner loop will incorporate a 'guardrail' module—a small, concurrently meta-trained critic network—that must approve proposed policy updates by evaluating them against learned safety, coherence, and performance regression metrics. The guardrail's veto will trigger a fallback to a more conservative, uncertainty-aware update from the offline prior, akin to constraints in offline RL algorithms like Conservative Q-Learning.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a framework for generating curricula of challenging preference shifts for robust meta-training, 2) a novel guarded adaptation mechanism that enforces safety during fast online learning, and 3) empirical validation in simulated and real robotic (SERL) tasks showing improved stability and sample efficiency when adapting to out-of-distribution human preferences.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning and offline RL for preferences exist, the integration of an active guardrail module within the adaptation loop and the use of adversarial preference curricula for meta-training are novel. This moves beyond mere adaptation speed to guarantee adaptation safety.

proposals.title: SimPLe++: Scaling Model-Based Offline Reinforcement Learning to Diverse Robotic and Simulated Domains
proposals.motivation: Model-based offline RL methods like SimPLe demonstrated strong sample efficiency on Atari, but their applicability to more complex, high-dimensional, and continuous control tasks remains an open question. Extending these methods is crucial for enabling data-efficient learning from diverse, pre-collected datasets (e.g., D5RL) in robotics and real-world settings, where online interaction is costly or unsafe.
proposals.hypothesis: The core principles of model-based offline RL can be scaled to complex robotic tasks by integrating learned dynamics models with expressive, scalable policy architectures like Q-Transformer and leveraging the structured action spaces common in robotics.
proposals.method: We will adapt the SimPLe framework, which iteratively learns a world model from offline data and uses it for policy optimization via planning or model-based RL. Key adaptations include: 1) using a latent dynamics model (e.g., RSSM) for pixel-based robotic tasks, 2) employing the Q-Transformer architecture for policy learning to handle multi-task, goal-conditioned, and high-dimensional action spaces, and 3) validating the approach on diverse offline datasets, including simulated robotic manipulation (e.g., Meta-World, D5RL Adroit) and real-world robot logs curated via the SERL infrastructure.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous benchmark for model-based offline RL beyond Atari, establishing its strengths and failure modes in continuous control. It will deliver open-source, scalable implementations and new datasets, advancing the practical application of offline RL for robotics.
proposals.novelty: While prior work applied model-based offline RL to discrete, low-dimensional action spaces (Atari), this proposal systematically investigates its extension to the high-dimensional, continuous, and structured action spaces characteristic of robotic control, integrating recent advances in scalable offline Q-learning.

proposals.title: MP3: Model-Predictive Planning with Progressive Refinement for Robotic Control
proposals.motivation: Learned video prediction models, such as those used in Finn's prior work on action-conditioned video prediction for robotic control, provide a rich, scalable world model for planning. However, standard planning methods like random shooting or Cross-Entropy Method (CEM) are inefficient, struggling with long horizons and complex tasks due to high-dimensional action sequences and poor sample efficiency. This limits the real-world applicability of model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) for robots.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical planning algorithm that uses a learned video model to first plan in a learned latent skill space, then refines these skills into precise actions, will significantly outperform flat action-space planners in sample efficiency and final task performance on long-horizon robotic manipulation tasks.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage planner. First, a high-level planner uses CEM to optimize over sequences of latent skill embeddings, using the video model to predict outcomes and compute rewards. Second, a low-level policy network, trained offline on diverse datasets (inspired by D5RL and Q-Transformer), translates each skill embedding into a short sequence of concrete actions. This decouples long-horizon reasoning from fine-grained control. We will integrate this into the SERL software suite and benchmark against standard CEM and offline RL baselines on Meta-World and real robotic tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, sample-efficient planning algorithm specifically designed for learned neural video models, advancing the frontier of model-based RL for robotics. It will demonstrate how to effectively combine the strengths of offline skill learning (from datasets) with online model-predictive control, enabling the solution of more complex, long-horizon tasks than previously possible.
proposals.novelty: While hierarchical RL and model-predictive control exist, the specific integration of skill-space planning with a learned video prediction model for *online* refinement is novel. This moves beyond using the model purely for data augmentation or training a flat policy, and beyond simple action-space CEM, by structuring the planning search space intelligently.

proposals.title: Latent Dynamics for Offline RL: Improving World Models with Unsupervised Temporal Priors
proposals.motivation: Offline reinforcement learning (RL) for robotics, as in D5RL and Q-Transformer, is fundamentally limited by the quality of its learned world models for planning and policy improvement. Current methods often learn dynamics models directly from task-specific reward signals or low-level states, which can be sample-inefficient and fail to capture the underlying structure of the environment. Integrating richer, task-agnostic temporal representations could lead to more robust and generalizable models, especially for real-world robotic tasks where data is scarce and diverse.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating unsupervised temporal representation learning objectives—inspired by recent advances in video prediction and masked autoencoding—into the dynamics model training process will yield latent spaces that better capture the essential, controllable factors of variation in robotic interaction, thereby improving the performance and sample efficiency of model-based offline RL algorithms.
proposals.method: We will extend the model-based offline RL framework by integrating a two-stage dynamics model. First, we will pre-train a latent dynamics model on large, unlabeled robotic datasets (e.g., D5RL) using a contrastive temporal loss (e.g., CPC or ST-DIM) and a masked autoencoder (MAE) reconstruction loss on sequences of observations and actions. This model will learn temporally coherent representations without reward labels. Second, we will fine-tune this pre-trained model within an offline RL algorithm like Conservative Q-Learning (CQL) or by integrating it into the planning backbone of Q-Transformer, using the learned latent space for more accurate and stable long-horizon value estimation and policy learning.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a principled method for injecting unsupervised temporal priors into model-based offline RL, demonstrably improving policy performance on benchmark suites like D5RL and real-robot tasks from SERL. It will provide new insights into the synergy between representation learning and sequential decision-making, potentially leading to more data-efficient and generalizable robotic agents.
proposals.novelty: While unsupervised representation learning is common in computer vision, its systematic integration as a temporal prior for *offline* model-based RL dynamics is novel. This approach moves beyond using representations for static perception and explicitly targets the learning of actionable dynamics models, a core challenge in Finn's research on sample-efficient robotic RL.

proposals.title: Meta-Adapt: Fast Adaptation of Video Prediction Models for Generalizable Robotic Task Planning
proposals.motivation: Video prediction models are powerful for learning world models and planning robotic tasks from offline datasets. However, they struggle to generalize to new environments (e.g., different video games or robotic scenes) without extensive fine-tuning data, which is costly to collect in the real world. Enabling rapid adaptation with minimal interaction is crucial for deploying versatile robotic agents in novel settings.
proposals.hypothesis: A video prediction model, meta-trained on a diverse offline dataset of robotic interactions and game playthroughs, can adapt its latent dynamics representation to a new, unseen environment using only a few frames of context, enabling effective task planning with minimal new data.
proposals.method: We will extend the Q-Transformer architecture by integrating a meta-learned, context-conditioned video prediction model as the dynamics backbone. Using an algorithm like Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML), we will train the model on a diverse, pre-2026 offline dataset (e.g., a combination of D5RL and game-engine benchmarks). The inner-loop adaptation will fine-tune a small set of context-modulation parameters or a hypernetwork based on a short sequence of frames from the new target environment. The adapted model will then be used for planning via autoregressive Q-value prediction.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a sample-efficient method for cross-domain model adaptation, directly reducing the real-world data needed for robotic skill transfer. It will demonstrate the synergy between meta-learning, offline world models, and goal-conditioned Q-learning, providing a new benchmark for fast adaptation in visual control tasks.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning and offline RL have been explored separately, their integration for few-shot adaptation of deep video prediction models for robotic planning is novel. This approach specifically targets the adaptation of the dynamics model itself, rather than just the policy, within a scalable offline RL framework like Q-Transformer.

proposals.title: World Models as Policy Distillation Engines: Efficient Model-Free Control via Latent Imagination
proposals.motivation: World models enable efficient planning and sample-efficient learning but are computationally expensive for real-time, low-latency control on physical robots. Conversely, model-free policies are fast but require vast amounts of real-world data. A critical open problem is how to leverage the rich understanding captured by a world model to synthesize a compact, performant, and fast model-free policy that can be deployed directly.
proposals.hypothesis: A world model, trained on diverse offline datasets, can serve as a high-quality, infinite source of synthetic experience for distilling a robust, lightweight, model-free policy that matches or exceeds the planner's performance while being orders of magnitude faster at inference.
proposals.method: We will build upon the Dreamer architecture and the Q-Transformer's scalable offline RL framework. First, we will train a latent world model (e.g., RSSM) on large, diverse offline datasets (e.g., D5RL). Then, instead of using it solely for planning, we will use it as a generative model of latent trajectories to train a small, autoregressive action-value function and a corresponding policy network via offline RL objectives within the model's imagination. The distillation loss will combine standard TD learning with a novel consistency loss that aligns the model-free Q-values with the planner's value estimates.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical pipeline for converting computationally intensive world models into deployable model-free agents, bridging the gap between planning-based and direct policy learning. It will provide a new methodology for leveraging offline datasets to train efficient real-time controllers, validated on real robotic tasks from the SERL suite.
proposals.novelty: While policy distillation and world models are known, the key novelty is framing the world model not as a planner but as a *generative teacher* for offline model-free RL within its own latent space, explicitly targeting the deployment efficiency gap in robotics, an underexplored dual to standard planning applications.

proposals.title: Continuous Action Tokenization for Vision-Language-Action Models
proposals.motivation: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, which map visual observations and language instructions to actions, have shown promise for robotic control. However, existing methods (e.g., RT-1, RT-2) primarily tokenize discrete or low-dimensional action spaces, limiting their applicability to complex, high-degree-of-freedom robots and continuous control tasks. Generalizing VLAs to handle high-dimensional continuous actions is a critical step toward scalable, data-driven robotic learning from diverse offline datasets.
proposals.hypothesis: By developing a novel, learnable tokenization scheme for continuous action vectors and integrating it with an autoregressive transformer architecture, we can create a VLA model that effectively generalizes across diverse, high-dimensional robotic manipulation tasks using offline data.
proposals.method: We will extend the Q-Transformer's autoregressive discretization approach to the full action space. A key innovation will be a Vector-Quantized Variational Autoencoder (VQ-VAE) trained to compress continuous action trajectories into a discrete token sequence, conditioned on visual and language embeddings. This action tokenizer will be integrated into a transformer decoder (similar in spirit to Q-Transformer but for direct action prediction) that autoregressively predicts action tokens from visual-language tokens. We will train and evaluate the model on large-scale, pre-2026 robotic datasets like D5RL and Bridge V2, focusing on tasks requiring precise, multi-dimensional control.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a general framework for applying VLA models to continuous control problems, significantly broadening their robotic applicability. It will yield a new, publicly released benchmark for evaluating VLAs on high-dimensional action spaces and demonstrate improved performance on complex manipulation tasks compared to prior discrete-action VLAs and conventional offline RL methods.
proposals.novelty: While prior work like Q-Transformer discretizes Q-values for offline RL, and RT-2 uses discrete action tokens, our method uniquely focuses on learning a direct, generative tokenization of the continuous action space itself, conditioned on multimodal inputs, for end-to-end VLA policy learning.

proposals.title: Embodiment-Agnostic Policy Learning via Morphology-Invariant Representations
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language-action (VLA) models and offline RL methods are typically trained and deployed on a single robot embodiment, limiting their generalizability and data efficiency. The field lacks robust methods for learning control policies that can transfer across diverse robot morphologies and actuator spaces, which is essential for scalable robotic learning and for leveraging heterogeneous datasets like those in D5RL. This work addresses the core challenge of embodiment generalization in robot learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by learning a shared, morphology-invariant representation space from offline datasets of diverse robots, a single autoregressive transformer model can generate effective, embodiment-conditioned action sequences for a wide variety of physical forms.
proposals.method: We will extend the Q-Transformer architecture to be embodiment-agnostic. The model will ingest robot state, images, and language instructions, alongside a learned morphology embedding vector that encodes the kinematic and dynamic properties of a specific robot. This embedding will condition all layers of the transformer, enabling it to modulate the Q-function and action predictions for the specific embodiment. We will train the model on a newly curated, multi-embodiment offline dataset, building on the D5RL and SERL frameworks, using a combination of offline RL and behavior cloning objectives.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel architecture for embodiment-conditioned offline RL, 2) a methodology and benchmark for cross-embodiment policy learning, and 3) a unified model capable of controlling diverse robots from a single set of weights, significantly advancing the scalability of VLA models.
proposals.novelty: While prior work in multi-task learning and domain adaptation exists, applying the transformer-based offline RL paradigm to explicitly condition on and generalize across fundamentally different robot morphologies and action spaces is a novel and unexplored direction for robust robotic control.

proposals.title: Hierarchical Offline Planning with Skill-Chaining Transformers
proposals.motivation: Current offline RL methods, including Q-Transformer, excel at learning goal-conditioned policies from diverse datasets but struggle with long-horizon tasks requiring multi-stage reasoning and precise temporal sequencing. For real-world robotics, such as complex assembly or kitchen tasks, the ability to decompose a high-level goal into a robust sequence of mid-level skills is critical for reliable autonomous execution. This gap limits the application of powerful offline models to truly complex, extended objectives.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchical model combining a high-level, diffusion-based skill planner with a low-level, autoregressive Q-function transformer will enable more robust long-horizon task decomposition and execution from offline datasets than flat, monolithic architectures.
proposals.method: We will extend the Q-Transformer architecture into a two-level hierarchy. The high-level planner will be a diffusion model trained on the D5RL dataset to output sequences of discrete skill embeddings, conditioned on the goal and initial state. The low-level executor will be a modified Q-Transformer, conditioned on the current skill embedding, to generate the precise actions for that skill. Training will use a modified conservative Q-learning objective at both levels, with skill-chaining consistency losses to ensure the output of one skill reliably sets up the initial state for the next.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, scalable hierarchical architecture for offline RL that explicitly addresses long-horizon planning. It will provide a new benchmark for complex, multi-stage task performance in offline settings, such as those in D5RL. The proposed method will demonstrate how to effectively chain learned skills from diverse datasets to achieve goals beyond the scope of any single trajectory.
proposals.novelty: While hierarchical RL and diffusion planners exist, their integration with conservative, offline Q-learning methods like Q-Transformer for skill-chaining is unexplored. The novelty lies in using a diffusion model over a learned skill embedding space specifically for robust, offline long-horizon planning, avoiding the compounding error issues of prior hierarchical methods.

proposals.title: Robo-Adapter: Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning of Vision-Language Models for Sample-Efficient Robotic Control
proposals.motivation: Large pre-trained Vision-Language Models (VLMs) like CLIP and Flamingo offer rich visual and semantic priors for robotics, but full fine-tuning is prohibitively expensive in compute and data, especially for real-world robotic tasks. This bottleneck limits the application of VLMs to domains with massive, diverse datasets. There is a critical need for adaptation strategies that can leverage these powerful models for specific robotic control policies with the sample efficiency characteristic of offline reinforcement learning.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods, specifically visual prompt tuning and low-rank adaptation (LoRA), into an offline RL framework, we can effectively adapt large VLMs for robotic control with significantly less task-specific data and compute than full fine-tuning, while preserving and utilizing the model's foundational knowledge.
proposals.method: We will build upon the Q-Transformer architecture, replacing its visual encoder with a frozen, pre-trained VLM (e.g., VC-1 or a CLIP variant). We will inject trainable parameters exclusively via visual prompt tokens prepended to the input sequence and via LoRA modules in the attention layers of the VLM. The Q-Transformer's autoregressive action-value head will be trained on diverse offline robotics datasets (e.g., from D5RL or RT-1), using conservative offline RL objectives to learn a control policy grounded in the adapted visual representations. We will systematically compare the data efficiency and final performance of this PEFT approach against full fine-tuning and training-from-scratch baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, open-source framework for sample-efficient VLM adaptation in robotics, demonstrated across several benchmark tasks. It will establish empirical guidelines on which PEFT strategies are most effective for robotic control and contribute a new, scalable pathway for integrating internet-scale pre-training into practical robotic learning systems.
proposals.novelty: While PEFT methods are known in NLP and vision, their application for adapting VLMs specifically within an offline RL control loop—combining the sample efficiency of offline RL with the parameter efficiency of PEFT—is unexplored. This differs from prior work like RT-2, which relies on full fine-tuning or co-finetuning of very large models.

proposals.title: World-Model-Augmented Vision-Language-Action Policies for Long-Horizon Robotic Manipulation
proposals.motivation: Current Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, which map high-dimensional observations and language instructions directly to actions, excel at short-horizon tasks but struggle with long-horizon reasoning and planning. This limits their applicability to complex, multi-step real-world robotic problems. Integrating the internal simulation and lookahead capabilities of model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) could address this fundamental limitation.
proposals.hypothesis: Augmenting a pre-trained VLA policy with a learned world model will enable more robust and sample-efficient long-horizon task execution by allowing the agent to internally simulate and plan over action sequences before committing to real-world execution.
proposals.method: We will build upon the Q-Transformer architecture, which provides a strong offline RL foundation for VLAs. We will train a latent dynamics model (e.g., using a variational autoencoder or Transformer) on the same diverse offline datasets (e.g., D5RL). This model will predict future latent states and rewards given the current state, a language goal, and a proposed action sequence. For online deployment, we will integrate the world model with the VLA policy using a planning algorithm such as Model Predictive Control (MPC) or Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) to refine the policy's initial action proposals.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a novel hybrid architecture that combines the generalization of large-scale VLAs with the deliberate planning of MBRL. It will provide a framework for improving the long-horizon reasoning of robotic agents, validated on complex, multi-stage manipulation benchmarks. The resulting method will advance sample-efficient real-world robotic learning by reducing trial-and-error in the environment.
proposals.novelty: While world models are common in pure RL, their integration into large pre-trained VLA policies for robotic control is largely unexplored. The key novelty is using the world model not as the primary policy, but as a planning module that refines and verifies the outputs of a data-driven VLA, creating a synergistic loop between fast pattern recognition and deliberate simulation.

proposals.title: Cross-Modal Alignment for Robotic Control: Offline Pre-training of Proprioceptive, Visual, and Language Representations
proposals.motivation: Current offline RL methods for robotics, such as those in D5RL and Q-Transformer, often struggle with generalization across diverse tasks and environments. A key bottleneck is the lack of a unified, semantically rich representation that aligns a robot's internal state (proprioception) with visual observations and high-level language instructions. Pre-2026 contrastive learning methods like CLIP have shown remarkable success in aligning vision and language, but this paradigm has not been systematically applied to ground low-level robot sensorimotor data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that contrastive pre-training on large-scale, unlabeled datasets of robot interaction videos paired with proprioceptive data and natural language descriptions will produce aligned embeddings that significantly improve the sample efficiency and generalization of downstream offline RL policies.
proposals.method: We will collect or utilize existing datasets (e.g., extended D5RL) containing tuples of robot proprioception, egocentric video, and natural language annotations or automatically generated captions. A multi-encoder architecture will be trained using a contrastive loss (e.g., InfoNCE) to maximize mutual information between proprioceptive, visual, and language embeddings. The frozen pre-trained encoders will then serve as a unified state representation for training offline RL agents, specifically testing with the Q-Transformer architecture on a suite of diverse, long-horizon manipulation tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a scalable method for learning semantically grounded robot state representations without task-specific rewards, 2) a demonstration that such pre-training substantially boosts the performance and generalization of state-of-the-art offline RL algorithms like Q-Transformer on sparse-reward, language-conditioned tasks, and 3) a new open-source multi-modal dataset for robotic representation learning.
proposals.novelty: While contrastive learning has been used for vision-language and vision-proprioception alignment separately, the integration of all three modalities—proprioception, vision, and language—into a single pre-training framework for offline RL is novel. This directly bridges the gap between high-level semantic understanding and low-level control, a core challenge in pre-2026 robot learning.

proposals.title: D4RL-WILDS: Self-Supervised Invariant Pre-Training for Robust Offline Robotic Reinforcement Learning
proposals.motivation: A core challenge in applying offline RL to real-world robotics is the domain shift between the training dataset and the deployment environment, such as changes in lighting, object appearance, or dynamics. The WILDS benchmark formalizes these distribution shifts, but robust methods for robotic RL remain underdeveloped. Learning representations invariant to these shifts from diverse, unlabeled offline datasets is a critical step toward deployable robotic agents.
proposals.hypothesis: Self-supervised pre-training objectives, applied to large-scale, diverse offline robot datasets (like D5RL), can learn visual and dynamic representations that are invariant to WILDS-style domain shifts. These invariant representations will significantly improve the robustness and sample efficiency of downstream offline RL policies, such as those trained with Q-Transformer, when faced with new environments.
proposals.method: We will extend the D5RL framework to include explicit WILDS-style domain annotations (e.g., different camera viewpoints or robot configurations). On this data, we will employ self-supervised learning (SSL) techniques like contrastive learning (SimCLR) or masked autoencoding (MAE) to pre-train an encoder. The key innovation is to structure the SSL tasks to enforce invariance across domains, for instance, by using data augmentation strategies that mimic the WILDS shifts or by employing domain-adversarial training within the SSL loss. The pre-trained encoder will then be frozen and used to provide features for training an offline RL agent, such as a Q-Transformer, on a downstream robotic manipulation task.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce: 1) a new benchmark combining D5RL's scale with WILDS' structured distribution shifts for robotic RL, 2) a methodology for SSL-driven invariant representation learning from offline robot data, and 3) empirical evidence that such pre-training substantially improves the zero-shot and few-shot adaptation performance of offline RL policies in novel domains.
proposals.novelty: While SSL and offline RL are both active areas, their combination for explicit domain-invariance in robotics is novel. Prior work like SERL focuses on online sample efficiency, and Q-Transformer scales offline RL but does not explicitly address domain shift. This proposal directly targets invariance learning from diverse offline data as a pre-training step for robust policy learning.

proposals.title: Proprioceptive Policy Execution: Robust Offline RL for Vision-Free Fine Manipulation
proposals.motivation: Robotic manipulation policies typically rely on high-bandwidth, closed-loop visual feedback, which is fragile to occlusions, lighting changes, and camera failures. For many contact-rich, fine manipulation tasks (e.g., insertion, assembly), the final execution phase is inherently governed by proprioceptive and tactile sensing. Developing policies that can execute robustly without visual feedback would increase reliability and enable deployment in visually degraded environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that offline reinforcement learning, trained on diverse datasets of successful manipulation with full state information, can learn robust policies that map from proprioceptive history to actions, effectively internalizing the necessary visual context for task completion and generalizing better to real-world visual perturbations than visuomotor policies.
proposals.method: We will leverage the D5RL framework to create or utilize existing datasets of dexterous manipulation tasks containing proprioceptive state, actions, and rewards. We will adapt the Q-Transformer architecture to operate on sequences of proprioceptive inputs (joint positions, velocities, end-effector pose, and optionally tactile readings) instead of images, using a transformer encoder to model temporal dependencies. Policies will be trained via conservative Q-learning offline, then evaluated in real-world robotic setups (using SERL infrastructure) under both normal and deliberately degraded visual conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a benchmark for proprioceptive-only policy learning from offline data, 2) a modified Q-Transformer architecture for high-dimensional proprioceptive sequence modeling, and 3) empirical evidence on the robustness and failure modes of vision-free execution compared to standard visuomotor policies for fine manipulation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses proprioception within larger state representations, the explicit focus on training policies to perform *complete* fine manipulation tasks *without any visual feedback at execution time* using scalable offline RL methods is novel. It directly challenges the assumption that dense visual servoing is always necessary for dexterous manipulation.

proposals.title: Unsupervised Policy Pre-training via Temporal Consistency and Goal Relabeling
proposals.motivation: Current methods for training robotic visual policies, such as those in D5RL and SERL, often rely on large datasets of expert trajectories with action labels. This trajectory supervision is expensive to collect and limits scalability to new tasks and environments. Removing this requirement would enable the use of vast, unlabeled video datasets of robotic interaction for pre-training more general and adaptable policies.
proposals.hypothesis: A CNN policy can be effectively pre-trained from unlabeled video sequences of robotic interaction by leveraging temporal consistency as a self-supervised learning signal and offline reinforcement learning objectives for temporal credit assignment.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage method. First, we pre-train a visual representation and a policy network on unlabeled interaction videos using a temporal cycle-consistency loss, where the policy must predict actions that align consecutive frames. Second, we fine-tune this pre-trained policy using offline RL algorithms, specifically an autoregressive Q-function like Q-Transformer, on smaller datasets with rewards. The key is to use goal-conditioned relabeling techniques, treating future states in the unlabeled sequence as goals to create a synthetic reward signal for pre-training.
proposals.contribution: This work would demonstrate a scalable path to pre-training robotic policies from passive video data without action labels. It would extend the applicability of offline RL methods like Q-Transformer to a broader class of datasets and contribute a new benchmark for unsupervised policy learning in robotics.
proposals.novelty: While self-supervised visual representation learning is established, directly learning a *policy network* without any action supervision is non-trivial. The novelty lies in combining temporal consistency objectives with goal-relabeling strategies within an offline RL framework to bootstrap policy learning from pure observation sequences.

proposals.title: Model-Free Guided Policy Search via Learned Value-Guided Diffusion
proposals.motivation: Guided Policy Search (GPS) is a powerful framework for training complex policies, but its reliance on an approximate dynamics model limits its applicability to real-world robotic tasks where such models are difficult or expensive to obtain. Recent advances in offline RL and large-scale robotic datasets, like those in D5RL, provide rich behavioral priors, but lack a principled way to perform trajectory optimization without a model. This work addresses the core challenge of performing guided policy search in a purely model-free, data-driven regime.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a learned value function, trained on large-scale offline robot data, can effectively guide a diffusion-based trajectory optimizer to generate high-reward target distributions, thereby enabling model-free GPS that matches or exceeds the sample efficiency and final performance of model-based counterparts.
proposals.method: We propose to replace the dynamics model in GPS with a learned value function, such as a Q-Transformer, trained on diverse offline datasets. This value function will guide a conditional diffusion model that generates target trajectory distributions. The policy will then be trained via supervised learning on these synthesized trajectories, with iterative refinement. The core algorithm will integrate SERL's infrastructure for efficient real-world robotic training loops, using the value-guided diffusion process as a substitute for model-based trajectory optimization.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a practical, model-free variant of GPS that leverages offline data for efficient online fine-tuning. It will extend the applicability of guided policy search to domains with complex, hard-to-model dynamics. We will provide an open-source implementation and benchmark results on real robotic manipulation tasks from the D5RL suite.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses models for guidance or diffusion for trajectory generation, the novel integration of a *value-guided* diffusion process as a direct, learned replacement for the dynamics model in the GPS framework is unexplored. This bypasses the need for explicit forward dynamics or inverse dynamics models entirely.

proposals.title: Scaling End-to-End Offline RL to Long-Horizon Robotic Tasks via Hierarchical Q-Transformers
proposals.motivation: While offline RL methods like Q-Transformer have shown success in short-horizon robotic manipulation, scaling end-to-end training to complex, long-horizon tasks (e.g., multi-stage kitchen chores) remains a critical open challenge. The compounding errors in value estimation and credit assignment over long sequences limit the applicability of current data-driven methods to real-world problems. This research directly addresses the core limitation preventing the deployment of learned policies for intricate, extended real-world operations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchical extension of the Q-Transformer architecture, trained end-to-end on large, diverse offline datasets, can effectively decompose long-horizon tasks and learn temporally abstract skills, thereby enabling robust performance on tasks with significantly greater complexity than previously demonstrated.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-level hierarchical Q-Transformer where a high-level model predicts subgoal embeddings over extended time intervals, and a low-level Q-Transformer learns to achieve these subgoals. The architecture will be trained end-to-end on existing datasets like D5RL and new long-horizon data collected via the SERL framework, using a modified temporal difference loss that operates across both levels. We will incorporate hindsight relabeling for the high-level subgoals to improve sample efficiency and task decomposition.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a scalable offline RL algorithm capable of learning from diverse data to solve long-horizon tasks, a significant step toward practical robotic learning. It will also contribute a new benchmark suite of long-horizon, multi-objective manipulation tasks to the community, facilitating future research in this direction.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the end-to-end, offline training of a hierarchical value-based architecture, combining the strengths of Q-Transformer's scalable multi-task learning with temporal abstraction, a combination not explored in pre-2026 work which typically trains hierarchies separately or uses online fine-tuning.

proposals.title: PlanFusion: Unifying Offline RL Policies with Learned Dynamics for Robust Long-Horizon Robotic Manipulation
proposals.motivation: Offline RL methods like Q-Transformer have shown success in learning expressive, goal-conditioned policies from diverse datasets. However, they can struggle with long-horizon tasks requiring precise sequencing or novel compositions of skills not explicitly present in the data. Conversely, model-based planning with learned dynamics is adept at long-horizon reasoning but often relies on simplistic, hand-designed action primitives or cost functions, limiting its ability to leverage complex, real-world demonstration data effectively.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a pre-trained, large-scale offline RL policy as a learned, temporally-extended action space within a model-based planning framework will significantly improve the robustness and long-horizon generalization of robotic manipulation, outperforming either approach in isolation.
proposals.method: We will first train a Q-Transformer-style model on a diverse offline dataset (e.g., D5RL) to produce a goal-conditioned, multi-skill policy. This policy will be used to generate candidate action sequences (or 'skill embeddings') over a short horizon. A separately trained, uncertainty-aware dynamics model will then predict the outcomes of these candidate sequences. A model-predictive control (MPC) planner will evaluate these outcomes against a task goal, selecting and executing the most promising skill sequence before re-planning. The system will be implemented and evaluated within the SERL software suite for real robotic validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel hybrid architecture that combines the data-driven richness of offline RL policies with the deliberative foresight of model-based planning. We expect to demonstrate superior performance on long-horizon, sparse-reward robotic tasks and provide a framework for injecting complex behavioral priors into planning systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has combined model-free RL with planning, our approach uniquely uses a large-scale, transformer-based offline RL policy not as a planner itself, but as a learned, high-level action generator for a downstream model-based planner, creating a synergistic hierarchy for long-horizon control.

proposals.title: Bridging Offline Datasets and Real-World Deployment: Imitation Learning for Robust, Multi-Task Robotic Policies
proposals.motivation: Large-scale offline datasets, such as those in D5RL, provide a rich foundation for learning robotic skills. However, directly applying offline RL methods like Q-Transformer to real robots remains challenging due to distribution shift and sample inefficiency. Imitation learning (IL) offers a more direct, stable learning paradigm from expert data, but its scalability to diverse, multi-task settings seen in offline datasets is under-explored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hybrid approach, using offline datasets to bootstrap robust multi-task policies via behavioral cloning and then fine-tuning them with interactive IL methods like DAgger on real hardware, will yield policies that are more sample-efficient and robust for real-world deployment than pure offline RL.
proposals.method: We will leverage the D5RL framework's diverse datasets to train an initial multi-task policy using Behavioral Cloning (BC) with a high-capacity CNN architecture. This pre-trained policy will be deployed on a real robot using the SERL software suite. To address distribution shift and improve robustness, we will employ the DAgger algorithm, where the robot executes the policy, a human expert provides corrective actions for encountered states, and the policy is iteratively fine-tuned on the aggregated dataset.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a practical pipeline for transferring large-scale offline datasets to real robotic control. It will provide a comparative analysis of BC, DAgger, and offline RL (Q-Transformer) in terms of real-world sample efficiency and task success. The resulting open-source policy and training logs within SERL will serve as a strong baseline for future work on sim-to-real and offline-to-online policy adaptation.
proposals.novelty: While DAgger and BC are well-known, their systematic integration with large-scale, diverse offline datasets (D5RL) and a modern robotic RL stack (SERL) to tackle multi-task real-world deployment is novel. This approach directly addresses the real-world applicability gap in the offline RL literature prevalent pre-2026.

proposals.title: Meta-Adapt: Fast Policy Adaptation to Novel Objects via Offline Meta-Reinforcement Learning
proposals.motivation: Real-world robotic deployment requires systems that can quickly adapt to novel objects or slight environmental variations not seen during training. While offline RL methods like those in D5RL and Q-Transformer enable learning from large, diverse datasets, they typically produce a single, static policy. This limits a robot's ability to handle new tasks or objects without costly online fine-tuning, which is sample-inefficient and impractical in many settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an offline meta-reinforcement learning (meta-RL) framework, trained on diverse offline datasets, can learn a policy initialization that enables fast, few-shot adaptation to novel objects and environments using only a small amount of interaction or demonstration data.
proposals.method: We will extend the Q-Transformer architecture within an offline meta-RL framework. The model will be meta-trained on the diverse, multi-task datasets from D5RL, treating each task (e.g., manipulating a different object) as a separate meta-training instance. We will employ a gradient-based meta-learning algorithm like MAML, where the inner-loop adaptation is performed via a few steps of offline fine-tuning or online interaction on a held-out 'adaptation' dataset for a novel object. The outer loop will optimize the initial Q-function parameters for rapid adaptation across the meta-training distribution.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a practical method for sample-efficient adaptation of robotic policies, bridging the gap between robust offline RL and flexible online adaptation. It will demonstrate empirically, using benchmarks from SERL and D5RL, that meta-learning over offline data significantly reduces the online interaction needed to master novel objects compared to standard offline-to-online fine-tuning.
proposals.novelty: While meta-RL and offline RL are established, their combination for robotic adaptation is nascent. The key novelty is applying gradient-based meta-learning specifically to large-scale, offline, multi-task datasets of robotic interactions (like D5RL) and leveraging the scalable, autoregressive Q-learning framework of Q-Transformer to enable adaptation at the level of discrete action sequences.

proposals.title: H-Transformer: Scaling Offline Visuomotor Learning to High-DoF Humanoid Robots
proposals.motivation: End-to-end visuomotor learning has shown promise for robotic manipulation, but scaling these methods to high-degree-of-freedom (DoF) humanoid robots remains a significant challenge. The complexity of humanoid dynamics, the curse of dimensionality in policy learning, and the scarcity of real-world humanoid data have prevented the application of powerful offline RL and imitation learning techniques. Success would enable more general and adaptive humanoid behaviors from visual input, a critical step toward versatile real-world robots.
proposals.hypothesis: That a scalable, transformer-based architecture for offline Q-learning, trained on diverse datasets of humanoid motion, can learn effective, stable visuomotor policies for high-DoF humanoid control directly from pixels.
proposals.method: We will extend the Q-Transformer architecture, which discretizes actions and uses an autoregressive model for Q-values, to the high-dimensional, continuous action space of a humanoid (e.g., 30+ DoF). We will combine large, diverse offline datasets—inspired by D5RL but for humanoids—including motion capture, simulated trajectories, and any available real robot data. The model will be a vision transformer (ViT) processing image observations, whose outputs are fused with proprioceptive state before being passed to the action-predicting Q-Transformer. Training will leverage conservative offline RL objectives to mitigate distributional shift.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) A novel, scalable neural architecture for high-DoF offline visuomotor control, 2) A publicly released, diverse humanoid dataset for offline RL (H-D5RL), and 3) Empirical validation on simulated and real humanoid platforms, demonstrating complex behaviors learned from pixels and offline data.
proposals.novelty: While Q-Transformer scaled offline RL for robotic arms, applying and adapting this paradigm to the full humanoid control problem, with its high dimensionality and balance constraints, is an unexplored and non-trivial extension. The integration of a unified visuomotor transformer for both perception and high-DoF action sequence modeling is novel.

proposals.title: Inverse Task Inference from Visual Demonstrations via Goal-Conditioned Policy Embeddings
proposals.motivation: Robotic policies trained on diverse, offline datasets (e.g., D5RL) can perform many tasks, but their intended goal during execution is often ambiguous from a single visual observation. This hinders human-robot collaboration, skill diagnosis, and the interpretability of learned behaviors. Inferring the underlying task from a policy's visual demonstration is a critical step towards robots that can explain their actions and align with human intent.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a goal-conditioned policy's internal representations, when combined with its visual observations, encode sufficient information to infer the intended task, and that this can be learned efficiently via contrastive learning on offline multi-task datasets.
proposals.method: We will leverage a pre-trained, multi-task Q-Transformer as a frozen policy backbone. Given a visual observation and the policy's action, we will train a lightweight inference network to predict a latent task embedding. This network will be trained via a contrastive objective, pulling together embeddings from trajectories of the same task in an offline dataset (e.g., D5RL variants) and pushing apart those from different tasks. The architecture will use a vision encoder (e.g., ResNet) and a small transformer to fuse visual features with the policy's Q-value or action history.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel method for task inference that is sample-efficient and compatible with existing offline RL policies. It will provide a new diagnostic tool for understanding what goal a robot is pursuing and a building block for hierarchical systems that can reason about sub-task completion.
proposals.novelty: While prior work infers goals from states or learns policies from goals, our approach uniquely inverts a *given* goal-conditioned policy to infer the task from visual demonstrations, using the policy itself as a rich, structured prior. This differs from standard inverse reinforcement learning by focusing on task identification rather than full reward reconstruction.

proposals.title: Multi-View Offline RL: Fusing Diverse Camera Streams for Robust Robotic Manipulation
proposals.motivation: Robotic manipulation in unstructured environments requires rich visual scene understanding to handle occlusions and viewpoint-dependent ambiguities. While prior offline RL methods like Q-Transformer and datasets like D5RL use single-camera inputs, real-world robotic setups (e.g., SERL) often deploy multiple cameras, yet their data is not jointly leveraged for policy learning. This creates a gap between data collection and policy architecture, limiting robustness.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an offline RL policy trained to actively fuse raw pixel inputs from multiple, uncalibrated camera views will achieve significantly greater robustness and task success rates in the presence of occlusions and viewpoint changes compared to policies trained on any single view or pre-fused features.
proposals.method: We will extend the Q-Transformer architecture to process multiple image streams. Each camera view will be encoded by a separate, shared-weight Vision Transformer (ViT). A cross-view attention module will fuse these token sequences before feeding into the Q-Transformer's autoregressive action prediction model. We will train and evaluate this system on new multi-view extensions of D5RL datasets and real robot data collected via the SERL suite, focusing on manipulation tasks with deliberate occlusions.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel architecture for multi-view offline RL, 2) a protocol and benchmark for multi-view robotic manipulation evaluation, and 3) empirical evidence that view-fusion within the policy network outperforms post-hoc data augmentation or feature averaging. It directly enhances the practicality of offline RL for real-world deployment.
