proposals.novelty: Unlike prior multi-view learning in robotics that often relies on calibrated systems or 3D reconstruction, our method learns a policy-centric fusion directly from raw, potentially uncalibrated views within a scalable offline RL framework, a combination not explored pre-2026.

proposals.title: Unsupervised Meta-Learning for Data-Efficient Robotic Skill Acquisition
proposals.motivation: Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) has shown promise for few-shot adaptation but relies on labeled tasks for meta-training, which is a bottleneck for robotics where labeled interaction data is costly. Extending MAML to leverage vast, unlabeled robotic interaction datasets (like those in D5RL) for meta-learning could enable robots to more efficiently acquire new skills from minimal downstream demonstrations or rewards. This directly addresses a core challenge in sample-efficient and offline robotic reinforcement learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a MAML-inspired framework can be trained in an unsupervised or semi-supervised manner on diverse, unlabeled interaction data, learning representations and initialization parameters that enable rapid adaptation to novel downstream tasks with very few labeled examples or reward signals.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage method building on Finn's expertise in offline RL and meta-learning. First, we will pre-train a neural network encoder and policy on diverse offline datasets (e.g., D5RL) using self-supervised objectives like temporal contrastive learning or reward prediction. Second, we will adapt the MAML outer-loop to perform meta-updates using these unsupervised objectives as proxy tasks, optimizing the model's parameters for fast adaptation. For semi-supervised settings, we will integrate a small set of labeled tasks with a larger corpus of unlabeled data during meta-training.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel unsupervised meta-learning algorithm that reduces reliance on labeled meta-training tasks, 2) an empirical evaluation in simulated and real robotic domains (leveraging SERL) demonstrating improved data-efficiency for new skill acquisition, and 3) a theoretical analysis connecting the self-supervised proxy tasks to adaptation performance.
proposals.novelty: While self-supervised learning and MAML are established separately, their combination for unsupervised meta-learning of robotic control policies is largely unexplored. The key novelty is reformulating the meta-learning objective around self-supervised losses derived from offline interaction data, rather than supervised task losses.

proposals.title: Task-Conditioned Offline RL with Cross-Attention Modulation
proposals.motivation: Prior work like D5RL and Q-Transformer has demonstrated the power of large, diverse offline datasets for robotic learning. However, effectively conditioning a single policy on a specific task from a multi-task dataset remains a challenge, especially when only a few demonstrations (a support set) define the target task. Current methods often use simple concatenation or hypernetworks, which may not efficiently extract and utilize relevant task context from high-dimensional demonstration data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using cross-attention mechanisms to modulate the layers of a base Q-function or policy network, using embeddings derived from a few-shot support set, will enable more precise and sample-efficient task conditioning in offline multi-task robotic reinforcement learning.
proposals.method: We will extend the Q-Transformer architecture, which models Q-values autoregressively. A demonstration encoder (e.g., a small Transformer) will process the few-shot support set of state-action trajectories. Its output embeddings will serve as keys and values for cross-attention layers inserted into the Q-Transformer's decoder blocks. These layers will modulate the network's internal representations, conditioning the Q-function on the task specified by the support set. The model will be trained offline on datasets like D5RL using conservative objectives to prevent out-of-distribution actions.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, attention-based architecture for task-conditioned offline RL that is more expressive than prior conditioning methods. It will provide a scalable framework for learning flexible policies from large, heterogeneous datasets, moving closer to generalist robotic agents that can adapt from minimal context.
proposals.novelty: While attention is common in vision-language models, its application as a direct, dense modulation signal for conditioning a robotic RL policy on a dynamic support set is novel. This differs from using attention only within the observation history or for simple goal embedding.

proposals.title: HIRL: Hierarchical Imitation and Reinforcement Learning for Long-Horizon Robotic Tasks
proposals.motivation: Current methods for long-horizon robotic tasks struggle to combine the abstract reasoning of large language models (LLMs) with the precise, low-level control needed for real-world execution. While LLMs can propose plausible plans, they lack grounding in specific robot capabilities and environmental affordances, and offline RL methods like Q-Transformer excel at learning individual skills but not at sequencing them over long horizons. A principled hierarchical framework is needed to bridge this gap for robust, generalizable task completion.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchical reinforcement learning (HRL) framework, with a high-level LLM-based planner trained via offline RL on diverse language-annotated datasets (like D5RL) and a low-level skill executor fine-tuned with sample-efficient online RL (via SERL), will significantly improve the success rate and generalization of long-horizon robotic manipulation tasks compared to flat LLM-to-policy or monolithic offline RL approaches.
proposals.method: We propose a two-level HRL architecture. The high-level policy is an LLM fine-tuned via offline reinforcement learning (using an autoregressive Q-function akin to Q-Transformer) on the D5RL dataset to output sequences of discrete, parameterized skill labels (e.g., 'pick', 'place'). The low-level policy consists of a library of continuous control skills, each initialized via offline RL from relevant subsets of D5RL and then adapted to a specific robot using the sample-efficient online RL techniques from SERL. The hierarchy is trained end-to-end by using the high-level policy's Q-values to guide updates to both levels.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, unified HRL framework that tightly integrates LLM-based abstract planning with grounded, efficient skill learning, 2) a new benchmark for evaluating hierarchical agents on long-horizon, language-conditioned robotic tasks, and 3) empirical validation showing improved sample efficiency and generalization over non-hierarchical baselines in real and simulated environments.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific integration of an LLM planner *trained as a high-level RL agent* using offline methods, rather than as a static planner, with a low-level that leverages both massive offline datasets and targeted online fine-tuning, creating a cohesive, end-to-end trainable HRL system for robotics.

proposals.title: AdaMoRep: Adaptive Motion Representation Selection for Efficient Robotic Video Learning
proposals.motivation: Robotic learning from video data, such as offline RL or imitation learning, requires efficient and robust motion understanding. Existing methods commit to a single representation (e.g., optical flow, latent dynamics models, or implicit neural fields) for all data, leading to inefficiency and brittleness across varied scenes. A context-aware system that dynamically chooses the best representation per instance could significantly improve the sample efficiency and computational cost of real-world robotic learning pipelines.
proposals.hypothesis: A lightweight, learned selector network can analyze contextual video features (e.g., motion magnitude, texture complexity) to dynamically route processing through the most computationally efficient and performant motion representation (explicit, implicit, or latent) for a given input, outperforming static representation models in task performance and efficiency.
proposals.method: We will develop a gated network with three expert pathways: an explicit flow estimator (RAFT), an implicit coordinate-based network (NeRF-style), and a latent dynamics model (trained via TD-MPC). A selector network, based on a small Transformer or CNN, will process spatio-temporal video patches and output a routing distribution. The system will be trained end-to-end on diverse robotic video datasets (e.g., D5RL, RoboNet) using a combined loss: a task loss (e.g., Q-Transformer's TD error for action prediction or future frame prediction) plus a Lagrangian penalty on the expected FLOPs of the selected pathway, encouraging efficient choices.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a versatile, adaptive model for motion understanding that improves the computational efficiency of video-based robotic learning. It will provide new insights into the relative strengths of different motion representations across visual contexts. The resulting software, integrated with the SERL suite, will enable more sample-efficient and scalable offline RL from diverse real-world video data.
proposals.novelty: While prior work compares representations statically, this proposes a dynamic, instance-adaptive selection mechanism trained jointly with task and cost objectives. It uniquely bridges explicit computer vision models, implicit neural representations, and latent RL dynamics models within a single, decision-making framework.

proposals.title: Curriculum-DPO: Progressive Alignment for Complex Deformable Object Manipulation
proposals.motivation: Deformable object manipulation (e.g., folding cloth, shaping dough) is a critical but unsolved challenge for real-world robotics, as it requires understanding complex, high-dimensional physical dynamics. While offline RL and preference-based learning (like DPO) offer promising data-driven solutions, they struggle with the long-horizon, multi-stage nature of these tasks. Current methods lack a structured mechanism to acquire foundational physical intuition before tackling advanced deformations, leading to inefficient learning and unsafe policies.
proposals.hypothesis: Structuring human preference data for Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) into a physically-grounded curriculum—progressing from rigid to elastic to plastic deformations—will enable more sample-efficient, performant, and aligned learning of complex deformable manipulation skills than applying DPO to a monolithic dataset.
proposals.method: We will leverage the SERL infrastructure for robotic RL and collect a diverse offline dataset (inspired by D5RL principles) of teleoperated deformable object interactions, annotated with human preferences for safety and effectiveness. A curriculum generator will partition this data by increasing physical complexity (e.g., moving a rigid block, then bending a rope, then folding a towel). At each curriculum stage, we will train an autoregressive Q-function (extending Q-Transformer architecture) via DPO, using the preference labels to align the policy. The policy from each stage bootstraps initialization for the next, progressively refining physical understanding.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a novel curriculum-DPO algorithm that integrates physical complexity into preference-based policy alignment, 2) a benchmark of deformable manipulation tasks with staged preference data, and 3) empirical validation on real robotic platforms showing improved sample efficiency and task success over non-curriculum DPO and standard offline RL baselines.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the synergistic fusion of a physically-meaningful curriculum with direct preference optimization, moving beyond using curricula for only task reward or abstract skill sequencing. This explicitly structures the preference learning problem itself according to the underlying continuum of material mechanics.

proposals.title: Dream2Act: Closing the Loop with Visual World Models for Real-Time Robotic Control
proposals.motivation: Recent advances in offline RL and world models, such as Q-Transformer and visual imagination models, have shown promise for learning from diverse datasets. However, these methods are typically evaluated in open-loop settings or require expensive online fine-tuning for real-world deployment. A critical gap exists in leveraging these learned models directly for reactive, closed-loop control in dynamic environments, which is essential for robust and generalizable robot behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a learned visual world model, trained on diverse offline robot data (e.g., D5RL), can be integrated into a real-time model-predictive control (MPC) framework to enable sample-efficient, performant, and robust closed-loop robotic manipulation without further online interaction.
proposals.method: We will build upon the Q-Transformer architecture and a separately trained video prediction model (e.g., a diffusion-based world model). The world model will generate imagined visual trajectories conditioned on candidate action sequences from an MPC planner. These imagined futures will be scored by the pre-trained Q-Transformer, providing a reward estimate for the MPC optimizer (e.g., Cross-Entropy Method). We will implement this system in the SERL software suite and evaluate it on real robotic tasks requiring precise, vision-based closed-loop correction, such as peg insertion and deformable object manipulation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a unified framework for offline model-based RL that bridges the gap between large-scale offline training and real-time control. It will demonstrate, both in simulation and on real robots, that learned models can be used for planning without online fine-tuning, advancing the goal of generalist robots that can adapt from static datasets.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses world models for planning in simulation or offline RL for open-loop policies, the non-obvious integration lies in using a pre-trained, autoregressive Q-function to evaluate *imagined* visual rollouts in a real-time MPC loop, creating a purely offline-trained visual model-predictive controller.

proposals.title: Self-Improving Video Filters: Bootstrapping Self-Supervised Learning from Uncurated Real-World Video
proposals.motivation: Scaling robotic learning to the real world requires vast, diverse video data, but raw video streams are dominated by uninformative frames (e.g., static scenes), wasting computation and slowing learning. Current self-supervised video methods assume pre-collected, curated datasets, which are expensive to create. This work addresses the core challenge of efficiently learning representations directly from continuous, uncurated video feeds, a prerequisite for lifelong robotic learning.
proposals.hypothesis: A self-improving, two-stage filtering pipeline can efficiently bootstrap high-quality visual representations from raw video by using the learning model's own uncertainty and training signals to iteratively refine its data selection criteria, mimicking ecological data-processing systems.
proposals.method: We propose a system with a fast, lightweight heuristic filter (using methods like optical flow magnitude or simple novelty detection) for initial clip selection. A primary model (e.g., a contrastive learner like MoCo or a temporal pretext task model) trains on this filtered data. We will then use the primary model's training dynamics—specifically, per-clip loss reduction and predictive uncertainty—as a reward signal to train a refinement filter via offline RL (leveraging algorithms like Conservative Q-Learning or IQL from the researcher's background). This refined filter will guide future data selection and trigger minimal human labeling for ambiguous clips, creating a feedback loop.
proposals.contribution: This will yield a principled framework for learning from continuous video with minimal human oversight, directly applicable to robotic data collection systems. It will advance the field by providing a method to automatically curate high-value training data from noise, improving sample efficiency for representation learning. The work will also demonstrate a novel application of offline RL for meta-learning over data streams.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is closing the loop where the representation learner actively improves its own data distribution, moving beyond static datasets or fixed heuristics. This adapts concepts from adaptive sensing in ecology to robotic vision, framing data selection as a learnable policy optimized for downstream learning efficiency.

proposals.title: V-ORL: Video-based Offline Reinforcement Learning from Uncurated Real-World Data
proposals.motivation: Current offline RL methods, like those in D5RL and Q-Transformer, rely on structured, action-annotated datasets which are expensive to collect and limit scalability. Meanwhile, the internet contains vast quantities of uncurated video depicting a wide range of physical tasks, from cooking to assembly. Leveraging this data could enable robots to learn foundational world models and skills without costly real-world interaction, directly addressing the data-scarcity bottleneck in robotic learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by combining large-scale video pretraining with inverse dynamics modeling and temporal difference learning, we can learn effective, generalizable value functions and policies from passive video observations alone, without action labels or reward annotations.
proposals.method: We will first pretrain a video transformer model on a large-scale dataset of egocentric and third-person task videos (e.g., Ego4D, Something-Something) using self-supervised objectives like masked autoencoding. We will then train an inverse dynamics model to infer latent actions between video frames. This model will be used to label a smaller, robot-specific video dataset with pseudo-actions. Finally, we will adapt the Q-Transformer architecture to train an autoregressive Q-function on this pseudo-labeled video dataset, using temporal consistency and future prediction as intrinsic rewards for offline RL fine-tuning.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel framework for converting passive video into a format suitable for offline RL, 2) a new benchmark for video-based offline RL derived from real-world video datasets, and 3) empirical evidence that value functions can be bootstrapped from observational data, reducing reliance on curated robotic demonstrations.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses video for representation learning or imitation, our approach uniquely focuses on learning *value functions* and *Q-functions* directly from uncurated video, framing it as a novel, scalable form of offline reinforcement learning without explicit rewards.

proposals.title: One-Shot Imitation from Videos: Learning Physical Interaction Policies with Minimal Data
proposals.motivation: Current offline and imitation learning methods for robotics, such as those in D5RL and Q-Transformer, require large, diverse datasets of expert demonstrations. However, collecting such data for every new physical task is prohibitively expensive. Enabling robots to learn from just one or a few video examples of a human performing a task would dramatically increase their adaptability and accessibility in unstructured environments.
proposals.hypothesis: By leveraging large-scale pre-trained video foundation models for robust spatio-temporal feature extraction and combining them with sample-efficient, offline RL algorithms, a robot can learn executable policies for novel physical interaction tasks from a single video demonstration.
proposals.method: We will build upon the Q-Transformer architecture for offline RL but condition the autoregressive Q-function on embeddings from a pre-trained video model (e.g., VideoMAE or a contrastive video-language model like CLIP). The policy will be trained in a two-stage process: first, large-scale but passive video data will pre-train a goal-conditioned policy in simulation via offline RL on datasets like D5RL. Second, we will fine-tune this policy in the real world using the SERL framework, where the reward function is derived from the similarity between the robot's observed trajectory and the feature embedding of the single target video.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new benchmark and method for one-shot visual imitation, pushing the frontier of data-efficient robot learning. It will demonstrate how to effectively bridge internet-scale video understanding models with sample-efficient robotic reinforcement learning, moving beyond the large-dataset paradigm of prior offline RL work.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses offline RL on large robot datasets or leverages video models for planning, the direct combination of a single video example with an offline Q-learning policy for precise physical control is unexplored. The key novelty is using the video model not for planning but to shape a dense reward signal for training a robust, executable policy with minimal real-world interaction.

proposals.title: Task-Aware Pre-Training for Efficient Offline Reinforcement Learning
proposals.motivation: The dominant paradigm in offline RL and robotic learning has been to train large, task-agnostic foundation models on massive, diverse datasets (e.g., D5RL). While powerful, this approach is computationally intensive and may be inefficient for specialized domains where data is structured around a known family of tasks. A targeted, task-specific pre-training strategy could offer a more sample- and compute-efficient path to high performance in such settings.
proposals.hypothesis: For a structured domain with a known distribution of related tasks, a model pre-trained on a curated, task-conditioned dataset will achieve higher downstream performance with fewer fine-tuning samples than a model pre-trained on a larger, undifferentiated dataset of the same total size.
proposals.method: We will design a controlled study using the D5RL and SERL frameworks. We will compare two pre-training strategies on a suite of simulated robotic manipulation tasks: 1) a task-agnostic baseline using a large, diverse offline dataset, and 2) a task-aware method where the offline dataset is structured and the model (e.g., a Q-Transformer variant) is pre-trained with explicit task conditioning or goal specification. Both models will then be fine-tuned on held-out tasks from the same domain using limited online interaction, with efficiency and final performance as key metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical evidence for when task-aware pre-training is beneficial, offering a practical alternative to the one-size-fits-all foundation model approach. It will yield concrete guidelines for dataset curation and algorithm design in sample-efficient robotic RL, potentially enabling effective learning in domains where collecting internet-scale diverse data is impractical.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on scaling data diversity for generality, we systematically investigate the counterpoint: strategically limiting and structuring pre-training data for efficiency within a target domain. This directly challenges the assumed necessity of task-agnosticism in foundational robotic models.

proposals.title: Continual Online Adaptation for Offline RL Policies via Experience-Driven Architecture Modulation
proposals.motivation: Current offline RL methods produce high-performing but static policies that cannot improve or adapt after deployment, limiting their robustness to distribution shifts and new tasks in real-world robotics. This creates a fundamental tension between the stability of offline training and the adaptability required for long-term autonomous operation. Enabling safe, efficient online learning post-deployment is a critical open challenge for making data-driven robotic policies truly practical.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a policy's neural network architecture can be selectively and sparsely modulated online based on new experience, enabling safe and sample-efficient adaptation without catastrophic forgetting of its offline-trained core skills. This architectural modulation, guided by uncertainty, provides a more stable and controllable substrate for continual learning than fine-tuning all parameters.
proposals.method: We will build upon the Q-Transformer architecture, freezing its core transformer blocks to preserve offline knowledge. We will introduce lightweight, sparsely activated adapter modules (e.g., hypernetworks or low-rank lateral connections) that are gated by an uncertainty metric computed from the offline dataset (inspired by D5RL's data diversity analysis). Online, a separate, small neural network will learn to activate specific adapters based on the current state's novelty, using a sample-efficient online RL algorithm (e.g., from the SERL suite) to update only the gating network and the activated adapters. This confines learning to a small, experience-dependent subset of parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for bridging offline and online RL that prioritizes stability. It will yield a concrete algorithm for continual robotic policy adaptation with empirical validation on real-world tasks. The research will also provide new insights into the relationship between architectural sparsity, uncertainty estimation, and safe online learning in deep networks.
proposals.novelty: While prior work combines offline pre-training with online fine-tuning, our approach is novel in using a data-dependent, dynamic architectural modulation as the primary mechanism for adaptation, deliberately constraining learning to a sparse, experience-selected parameter subspace. This moves beyond simply tuning the entire network or using conservative regularization.

proposals.title: Scaling Laws for Generalist Robotic Control: Investigating the Joint Scaling of Model and Data in Offline RL
proposals.motivation: Recent offline RL methods like Q-Transformer and large-scale datasets like D5RL have demonstrated promising results in robotic manipulation. However, the scaling properties of these systems—how performance improves with increased model capacity and dataset diversity/volume—remain poorly understood, limiting our ability to build truly generalist robotic agents. Establishing these scaling laws is critical for efficiently allocating computational and data-collection resources to achieve robust, multi-task robotic control.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that offline RL performance on a broad suite of robotic manipulation tasks will follow predictable power-law scaling with respect to both model parameter count and the volume/diversity of training data, and that scaling both in tandem is necessary to unlock emergent generalization to novel tasks.
proposals.method: We will conduct a systematic scaling study using the Q-Transformer architecture as a base, scaling its Transformer backbone from tens of millions to several billion parameters. In parallel, we will scale the training data by massively expanding the D5RL suite with new tasks, embodiments, and visual domains using the SERL infrastructure for efficient real-world data collection. We will analyze performance trends across held-out tasks and environments to fit scaling laws and identify bottlenecks.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first empirical scaling laws for offline RL in robotics, providing a quantitative framework for future research and development. It will also release the largest and most diverse public benchmark for robotic offline RL, alongside a family of scaled generalist Q-Transformer models, setting a new state-of-the-art for multi-task control.
proposals.novelty: While scaling laws are studied in language and vision, their systematic investigation in robotic offline RL, where action sequences and multi-modal data interact, is novel. This work uniquely focuses on the *joint* scaling of model and robotic-specific data diversity, moving beyond simple dataset size.

proposals.title: Temporal Abstraction for Long-Horizon Tasks via Offline Skill Chaining and Goal-Conditioned Planning
proposals.motivation: Current offline RL methods, including Q-Transformer, struggle with long-horizon robotic tasks due to compounding errors over extended timeframes and the difficulty of credit assignment. Real-world tasks like multi-step assembly or kitchen chores require temporal abstraction, but existing methods lack a principled way to discover and chain reusable skills from static, diverse datasets like those in D5RL.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an offline method combining unsupervised skill discovery with a high-level, goal-conditioned planner can effectively decompose long-horizon tasks, leading to more robust and sample-efficient execution on real robots compared to flat, monolithic policies.
proposals.method: We will extend the Q-Transformer architecture by first learning a library of temporally extended skills via a variational objective on the D5RL datasets, grounding skills in the latent space of a goal-conditioned Q-function. A high-level transformer-based planner will then sequence these skills by predicting subgoals and skill durations, trained entirely offline using hindsight relabeling. The full hierarchical policy will be fine-tuned and evaluated in the SERL framework on long-horizon robotic manipulation benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, fully offline hierarchical RL framework that enables long-horizon planning from diverse datasets. It will provide new benchmarks for hierarchical offline RL and demonstrate improved success rates on complex, multi-stage real-robot tasks compared to flat offline RL baselines.
proposals.novelty: While prior work combines skills and planning, our approach is uniquely designed for the offline setting, learning both the skill library and the skill-chaining planner jointly from static data without online exploration, a key challenge pre-2026. The integration of duration-aware skill selection within the Q-Transformer architecture is also novel.

proposals.title: Few-Shot Policy Adaptation via Demonstration-Conditioned Q-Learning and In-Context Skill Retrieval
proposals.motivation: Current offline RL and imitation learning methods require large, task-specific datasets, limiting their applicability to new robotic tasks where data collection is expensive. Achieving robust adaptation with only a handful of demonstrations per task is a critical step towards generalist robots that can learn from minimal human input. This directly addresses a key bottleneck in deploying learned policies in real-world, variable settings.
proposals.hypothesis: By combining a large, diverse offline dataset of robotic behaviors with a demonstration-conditioned Q-function architecture, a policy can perform in-context retrieval and composition of relevant skills, enabling effective adaptation to novel tasks from fewer than five demonstrations.
proposals.method: We will extend the Q-Transformer architecture to be conditioned on embeddings of a few demonstration trajectories, enabling the Q-function to modulate its predictions based on the target task. A contrastive objective will align the demonstration embedding space with the state-action value space of the offline dataset (e.g., D5RL). At adaptation time, the policy will perform in-context skill retrieval by attending to the most relevant transitions from the offline dataset, guided by the demonstration embedding, and fine-tune only a small adapter network.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for extreme data-efficient robotic adaptation, demonstrating state-of-the-art few-shot performance on Meta-World and real-robot benchmarks. It will provide new insights into how demonstration context can guide value function generalization and in-context skill reuse from heterogeneous offline data.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses demonstrations for policy initialization or reward shaping, our approach uniquely integrates them as a conditioning signal for a scalable offline RL value function, enabling dynamic skill retrieval without policy re-training for each new task.

proposals.title: Passive World Models (PaWM): Learning Actionable Dynamics from Unlabeled Video via Inverse Dynamics and Temporal Contrast
proposals.motivation: Scaling robotic learning requires vast, diverse datasets, but current offline RL methods like those in D5RL and Q-Transformer rely on action-labeled trajectories, which are expensive to collect. Passive video data (e.g., from human YouTube videos or robot teleoperation archives) is abundant but lacks action labels, making it unusable for standard world model or policy learning. Bridging this gap is crucial for leveraging internet-scale passive data for robotics.
proposals.hypothesis: A world model that jointly learns a temporally consistent latent state representation and an implicit inverse dynamics model can be trained effectively from passive, unlabeled video, and this model can serve as a powerful, data-efficient prior for downstream robotic reinforcement learning tasks.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage self-supervised framework. First, we train a video prediction model (e.g., a Transformer-based autoregressive architecture) using a temporal contrastive loss (like Time Contrastive Networks) to learn latent embeddings that encode object-centric states and dynamics. Second, we train an implicit inverse dynamics head within this latent space to predict the *distribution* of actions that could explain state transitions, using the uncertainty from this distribution to guide exploration. The final model will be fine-tuned and evaluated within the SERL framework for sample-efficient robotic RL on manipulation tasks from the Meta-World benchmark.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel method for learning actionable world models from purely passive video, 2) a new, large-scale simulated benchmark of passive human/robot videos for controlled evaluation, and 3) a demonstration of how such models accelerate downstream policy learning in sample-efficient robotic RL, reducing reliance on expensive action-labeled datasets.
proposals.novelty: While prior work on video prediction or contrastive learning ignores actionability, and inverse dynamics models require action labels, our key novelty is the joint, self-supervised learning of dynamics and an *implicit* inverse model directly from pixels, making passive observation directly useful for control.

ideas.title: Curriculum Learning for Deformable Object Manipulation via Direct Preference Optimization
ideas.description: This research direction combines curriculum learning for DPO with models for deformable object interaction. It structures a curriculum where preference data for DPO training is organized by increasing physical complexity, starting with simple rigid interactions and progressing to challenging tasks involving stretching, bending, or fracturing materials. At each stage, DPO is used to align the agent's policy with human preferences for safe, effective, and physically plausible manipulation of the deformable objects. This synergistic approach enables the progressive learning of complex, real-world physical skills that are both performant and aligned, which neither curriculum learning on abstract tasks nor a static model of deformation could achieve alone.

ideas.title: Adapting Inverse Reinforcement Learning from Robotics for Trajectory-Level DPO in Dialogue
ideas.description: The original idea seeks to adapt Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) for aligning model behavior across entire conversational trajectories, not single responses. Drawing an analogy from Robotics and Control Theory, specifically Inverse Reinforcement Learning (IRL), which solves the problem of inferring a reward function from observed optimal agent trajectories. The analogous IRL solution handles credit assignment over sequences of states and actions. This solution is adapted by treating a preferred multi-turn dialogue as a demonstrated trajectory. The research idea is to develop a trajectory-level DPO variant that optimizes model policy based on preferences between complete conversation histories, inferring the latent user preferences that govern long-term interaction quality, thus solving the credit assignment problem for conversational alignment.

ideas.title: Unsupervised Video Representation Learning via Self-Supervised Filtering
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from ecological data processing to the problem of learning from uncurated real-world video. In ecology, vast streams of uncurated sensor data are managed through automated trigger systems and collaborative filtering models to focus computational resources on salient events. The adapted idea proposes a two-stage, self-improving pipeline for video: initial fast heuristics perform a first-pass filter on raw video streams to select clips with high learning potential (e.g., containing action, object interactions, or novel scenes). A primary self-supervised learning model is then trained on this filtered data. Crucially, the model's learning signals and its own uncertainty are used to iteratively refine the initial filtering heuristics and to selectively query a minimal human oracle, creating a feedback loop that steadily increases the quality and efficiency of learning from inherently noisy, large-scale video data.

ideas.title: Context-Adaptive Hybrid Motion Representation Learning
ideas.description: This research investigates a context-adaptive framework that dynamically selects between explicit, implicit, and latent motion representations on a per-instance basis. A lightweight selector network analyzes local and global video context—such as motion magnitude, texture, and occlusion patterns—to route processing through the most suitable representation pathway. The system is trained with a combined objective of task performance (e.g., prediction, interpolation) and a penalty on computational cost, encouraging efficient, content-aware decisions. This approach moves beyond a static comparison to create a robust, versatile model that leverages the strengths of each representation type where they are most effective.

ideas.title: Online Contrastive DPO with Adaptive Hard Negative Mining
ideas.description: This research develops an online DPO framework that integrates contrastive learning to dynamically improve its own training data quality. As new preference pairs are collected from interactions with the environment or a human, the system employs hard negative mining to select the most challenging or informative dispreferred responses from a candidate pool, including past policy outputs. This adaptive negative selection, powered by noise-contrastive estimation, focuses the policy updates on the most discriminative comparisons, making each online learning step more efficient. Consequently, the method enables faster convergence and more robust policy improvement in iterative settings like RLHF from human feedback or online dialogue, where the data distribution continuously evolves.

ideas.title: Bayesian Nonparametric Preference Learning for Robust Alignment
ideas.description: This research direction develops a Bayesian nonparametric approach to preference-based reinforcement learning (PbRL) for AI alignment. It replaces the standard Bradley-Terry model assumption with a flexible, data-driven prior over possible preference probability functions, such as a Gaussian process. Bayesian inference is then used not only to quantify uncertainty in policy updates given a model, but also to represent epistemic uncertainty about the correct form of the preference model itself. This allows the agent to more robustly learn from inconsistent or limited human feedback, actively query for informative comparisons to reduce model ambiguity, and avoid overconfident policy optimization based on an incorrectly assumed preference structure.

ideas.title: Robust Preference Adaptation via Guarded Meta-Learning with Simulated Preference Shifts
ideas.description: This approach meta-trains a model not just for speed, but for robustness when adapting to novel preferences. The outer meta-training loop uses a dynamically generated curriculum of simulated preference distributions, including contradictory preferences and complex trade-off scenarios, to build more general adaptation priors. During fast adaptation to a new individual or group, an embedded guardrail module actively evaluates each proposed policy update against a set of safety, coherence, and minimal-regression criteria before it is applied. This results in a system that can adapt quickly from few examples while being inherently cautious and stable, even when faced with preferences significantly different from its core training data.

ideas.title: Online DPO via Adaptive Bayesian Filtering
ideas.description: This idea develops an online version of Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) by drawing an analogy from adaptive Bayesian filtering in control theory and signal processing. In that field, systems like Kalman filters recursively update a state estimate by combining a prior prediction with new, noisy observations. Adapted to DPO, the policy's parameter distribution is treated as the evolving state. As new batches of preference data are collected, a Bayesian update step is performed, using the current policy as the prior and the new preference comparisons to compute a likelihood. This results in a computationally efficient, recursive online learning algorithm that continuously refines the policy with incoming data, eliminating the need for periodic full retraining on an ever-growing dataset and allowing real-time adaptation to evolving human feedback.

ideas.title: Data-Driven Preference Model Learning for Alignment
ideas.description: This research develops a practical framework for alignment that learns the human preference model directly from interaction data, eliminating the need for a pre-specified parametric assumption like Bradley-Terry. The core is a model-agnostic training system that uses expressive neural networks to estimate pairwise preference probabilities or full ranking distributions, conditioned on the context and the items being compared. The approach includes rigorous benchmarking protocols to test the learned model's generalization under distributional shift, ensuring robustness. This results in alignment methods that are both more flexible to real human preference structures and concretely implementable.

ideas.title: Learning Distributed Preference Manifolds for Robust Large Model Alignment
ideas.description: This research addresses the fundamental limitation of DPO's monolithic preference objective by developing a two-stage, memory-efficient framework for aligning trillion-parameter models. First, it trains a scalable, distributed ensemble of lightweight preference heads that collaboratively map responses to a multi-dimensional preference manifold, capturing the inherent complexity and contradictions in human feedback. Second, it uses this manifold to regularize policy training via constrained optimization or gradient-based guidance, ensuring the model remains robust across diverse preference trade-offs without overfitting. The system is designed for distributed execution, separating the high-memory preference modeling from the policy network, enabling efficient scaling while producing more generalized and reliable aligned models.

ideas.title: Dynamic Synthetic Preference Engines for Adaptive Alignment
ideas.description: This research investigates the design of AI-driven preference engines that generate synthetic training data for Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), where these engines explicitly model the temporal dynamics and context-dependence of human values. Instead of using a fixed dataset or a static AI labeler, the system employs meta-models that simulate how preferences might evolve, shift in different scenarios, or respond to new information, creating a dynamic stream of preference pairs. This allows for the training of language models that are not just aligned to a past snapshot, but are inherently adaptive and robust to changing human judgments, effectively using synthetic sources to solve the core challenge of non-stationarity in alignment.

ideas.title: Uncertainty-Aware Imagination with Divergence Monitoring for Robust Closed-Loop Control
ideas.description: This research develops a closed-loop control system that uses a learned visual imagination model for planning, but crucially incorporates a parallel mechanism to monitor and correct for prediction-reality divergence. A secondary model continuously estimates the uncertainty and discrepancy between imagined and observed state transitions. When divergence is low, the system follows the imaginative plan for efficient, long-horizon control. When high divergence is detected, it triggers targeted re-planning, online model adaptation, or a switch to a guaranteed-safe backup policy, preventing catastrophic failure due to model inaccuracies.

ideas.title: Self-Supervised Preference Distillation for Cross-Modal Direct Preference Optimization
ideas.description: This approach overcomes the data bottleneck of multimodal DPO by automating preference labeling. First, a compact but high-quality human feedback dataset trains a cross-modal preference predictor model. This predictor then scores millions of pairs of model-generated outputs (images, trajectories, etc.) to create a large, synthetic preference dataset. The target model is then aligned using standard DPO on this distilled dataset, enabling scalable and robust preference learning without continuous expensive human annotation.

ideas.title: Curriculum DPO via Cognitive Load Sequencing
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from Educational Psychology, specifically cognitive load theory and scaffolding, to the DPO training paradigm. The analogous problem is teaching a complex skill to a human learner without overwhelming them. The solution is to sequence training material by difficulty, starting with foundational concepts. Adapted to DPO, we would structure the preference dataset into a curriculum based on the estimated complexity of the alignment judgment required. Initial training phases use clear, high-margin preference pairs (e.g., helpful vs. harmful, concise vs. rambling) to efficiently establish a strong base reward model within the policy. Subsequent phases gradually introduce more challenging distinctions, such as trade-offs between helpfulness and verbosity, subtle safety nuances, or contextual ethical reasoning. This curriculum learning approach for DPO aims to train more complex and robust aligned behaviors by managing the 'optimization load' on the policy, leading to better convergence and final performance than training on a mixed-difficulty batch.

ideas.title: Synthetic Preference Generation via a 'Synthetic Ecology' of AI Models
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that preference data for Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) must come from a single, high-quality human or AI source. Drawing an analogy from computational ecology, where 'synthetic ecologies' of digital organisms with simple rules interact to produce complex evolutionary dynamics, we propose creating a 'synthetic preference ecology'. This involves simulating a multi-agent environment populated by diverse AI models or automated rule-systems, each generating preferences or critiques over language model outputs. Their interactions—through debate, voting, or fitness-based selection—produce emergent, consensus preference labels. This adapted solution uses the dynamics of this synthetic system as a robust, automated, and diverse source of preference data for DPO training, mitigating bias from any single non-human source and exploring the stability and quality of preferences generated through simulated interaction.

ideas.title: Bayesian Active Perception for Preference-Aware Egocentric Forecasting
ideas.description: This research develops a unified Bayesian framework for action-conditioned egocentric video prediction where the camera's motion is actively controlled to resolve uncertainty about a user's latent preferences. The model maintains a posterior distribution over user preferences, inferred from their reactions to predicted futures, and uses this uncertainty to guide an active perception policy for the camera. This allows the system to strategically explore the visual environment, capturing viewpoints that are most informative for disambiguating user goals, leading to highly personalized and reliable long-horizon forecasts from a dynamic first-person perspective.

ideas.title: Meta-Learning for Preference Adaptation via Immune System-Inspired Affinity Maturation
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from computational immunology. The core challenge—fast adaptation to a novel signal (a new user's preferences) using a meta-trained foundation—parallels how the adaptive immune system performs affinity maturation to generate specific antibodies for a new pathogen. The analogous solution involves a pre-existing diverse repertoire and a rapid, mutation-and-selection process. Adapted to the ML context, we propose to meta-train a model using DPO not just for general adaptability, but to explicitly structure its parameter space into a repertoire of learnable preference patterns. Upon few-shot exposure to a new individual or group, the model initiates a fast, gradient-based 'affinity maturation' loop. This inner loop performs constrained exploration (akin to hypermutation) within the activated preference subspace, with the DPO loss acting as the selection mechanism to reinforce responses that have high 'affinity' for the new preference data. This biologically-inspired mechanism aims to achieve more efficient and robust few-shot preference adaptation than standard meta-learning fine-tuning.

ideas.title: On-Device, Data-Efficient Multimodal DPO for Edge Alignment
ideas.description: This research fuses lightweight DPO and multimodal DPO to create a framework for directly aligning small generative models (for images, audio, or embodied actions) on resource-constrained edge devices. The core challenge is developing data-efficient and computationally tractable variants of DPO that operate with the limited preference pairs a device can collect from a single user or environment. The resulting capability allows for continuous, private adaptation of a model's outputs—like a smartphone's image stylizer or a household robot's behavior—to individual user tastes or local context without cloud dependency, merging the efficiency of on-device tuning with the generality of preference alignment beyond text.

ideas.title: Apply Geophysical Tomography to Inverse RL for Multi-Factor Reward Recovery
ideas.description: This idea frames Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) as an inverse reinforcement learning problem, drawing a direct analogy to geophysical tomography. In tomography, a complex internal structure (like Earth's subsurface) is inferred from sparse, indirect surface measurements. Similarly, we propose to treat pairwise human preferences as sparse, indirect measurements of a latent, multi-dimensional reward structure. The adapted solution is to develop a tomographic inverse model for DPO that recovers a decomposed reward function—represented as a vector or manifold in a latent space of attributes (e.g., factuality, conciseness, tone)—rather than a single scalar. This involves using regularization techniques from inverse problems (like smoothness or sparsity constraints) on the reward manifold to find the most plausible multi-factor reward model that explains all observed preferences, thereby extracting more nuanced and interpretable reward signals from the same preference data.

ideas.title: Robust On-Device Preference Tuning with Adaptive Anchoring and Noise Resilience
ideas.description: This system enables safe and effective personalization of small models on edge devices by directly addressing the instability of learning from limited, noisy preference data. It augments a lightweight DPO objective with an adaptive anchoring mechanism that constrains updates to prevent catastrophic forgetting, where the strength of the anchor is tuned by a learned confidence estimator for each local data point. The approach further stabilizes training by generating synthetic, in-context preference pairs from the device's own interaction history to improve the contrastive signal. This results in a robust tuning procedure that personalizes the model effectively while preserving its core capabilities and generalizing better from sparse user feedback.

ideas.title: Scale task complexity to unstructured environments

ideas.sourceTitle: Learning Fine-Grained Bimanual Manipulation with Low-Cost Hardware

ideas.title: Scale up to multi-robot collaborative fine manipulation
ideas.description: Coordinate more than two low-cost arms to perform complex assembly or packaging tasks.
ideas.sourceTitle: Learning Fine-Grained Bimanual Manipulation with Low-Cost Hardware

ideas.title: Robust Calibrated Contrastive Preference Optimization with Ambiguity Filtering
ideas.description: This method enhances DPO by integrating a contrastive learning objective that is robust to noise and ambiguity in human preference data. A lightweight auxiliary network is trained concurrently to estimate the confidence or potential error in each preference pair, filtering out ambiguous examples. The core DPO loss is then combined with a calibrated contrastive loss that only operates on high-confidence pairs, with adaptive margins. This approach improves learning efficiency and sample quality while preventing overfitting to dataset errors, leading to more reliable and generalizable policy alignment.

ideas.title: Conflict-Aware Gradient Modulation for Stable Joint DPO and Supervised Fine-Tuning
ideas.description: This redesigned approach tackles the fundamental problem of gradient conflict in multi-loss alignment training. It integrates a real-time, parameter-level gradient router that detects and mitigates destructive interference between the supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) objectives. The system dynamically prioritizes gradients to preserve task-specific accuracy while allowing preference learning to guide the model where signals are complementary. This results in a more stable and effective training process that robustly achieves both high capability and strong alignment.

ideas.title: Bayesian Adaptive Psychophysics for Preference Learning
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to a core problem in psychophysics—efficiently estimating a latent threshold from noisy binary responses—to the domain of learning user preferences. The analogous field uses Bayesian adaptive testing (e.g., QUEST algorithms), where a prior over the threshold is updated after each trial, and the next stimulus is chosen optimally to reduce uncertainty. Adapted here, a Bayesian model quantifies uncertainty over a user's latent preference parameters. Following each feedback event, the posterior is updated, and the system's policy for generating the next query or action is explicitly designed to maximize expected information gain (e.g., via Bayesian Active Learning). This creates a principled, uncertainty-aware loop that minimizes the number of interactions needed to learn a precise preference model, directly transferring the efficiency and robustness of psychophysical methods to preference-based learning systems.

ideas.title: Scale down to minimalist embodiment
ideas.description: Apply the method to a robot with very limited actuators and sensors.
ideas.sourceTitle: Do As I Can, Not As I Say: Grounding Language in Robotic Affordances

ideas.title: Online Trajectory-Aware DPO for Adaptive Dialogue Agents
ideas.description: This research develops an online variant of Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) specifically designed for multi-turn dialogue alignment. The method updates the policy incrementally using preference data collected from entire conversational trajectories, not just isolated responses. This enables the model to learn complex, cooperative behaviors and strategic discourse patterns through sequential interaction. The core innovation is a computationally efficient mechanism that leverages trajectory-level preferences for online updates, allowing the agent to adapt its long-term conversational strategies in real-time as it interacts with users.

ideas.title: Scale environment complexity dynamically
ideas.description: Automatically generate tasks of increasing physical or perceptual complexity within the Meta-World framework.
ideas.sourceTitle: Meta-World: A Benchmark and Evaluation for Multi-Task and Meta Reinforcement Learning

ideas.title: Anchored Preference Synthesis with Meta-Critic Arbitration for DPO
ideas.description: This approach mitigates the risk of value collapse in synthetic-preference DPO by integrating a small set of high-quality human preference judgments as fixed anchors. A separate meta-critic model is trained to predict these human choices from synthetic preference pairs. During DPO training on the main model, this meta-critic dynamically assigns credibility weights to each synthetic preference datum, down-weighting those it predicts a human would disagree with. The system thus leverages scalable synthetic data while being continuously corrected and grounded by a sparse human signal, preserving alignment to human values.

ideas.title: DPO with Implicit Reference Regularization via Surrogate Model Distillation
ideas.description: This method enables DPO-style alignment without live access to the original reference model by first distilling a surrogate model. The surrogate is trained offline on a fixed dataset of (prompt, reference model completion) pairs to mimic the reference model's log-probability distribution. During subsequent DPO training on human preferences, the policy model is regularized against this frozen surrogate, which acts as a stable anchor preventing reward hacking. The approach maintains the computational benefits of omitting a live reference model while preserving the crucial regularization needed for stable alignment.

ideas.title: Uniform gradient importance across tasks

ideas.title: Adapting Molecular Dynamics Potentials for Multi-Object Interaction Modeling
ideas.description: This research seed proposes enhancing a model to handle complex scenes with multiple interacting objects and collisions. The analogy is drawn from computational chemistry and molecular dynamics, where the complex, many-body problem of molecular interactions is solved by defining a total potential energy function (a force field) as a sum of simpler, interpretable terms for bonded, non-bonded, and angular interactions, with system evolution computed via force integration. The adapted idea is to design a neural model that explicitly represents multi-object interactions as a composition of learnable, physics-inspired potential terms (e.g., for proximity, contact, and momentum). The model would predict the parameters of these interpretable interaction components from object states, allowing it to generalize systematically to varying numbers of objects and novel object types, thereby robustly simulating collisions and complex interactions.

ideas.title: Curriculum-Guided Multi-Objective Alignment (CuMOA)
ideas.description: This method structures both preference data and supervised task data into a unified difficulty curriculum. Training begins with easy, unambiguous examples where supervised fine-tuning (SFT) for task accuracy and Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) for alignment are jointly applied. The curriculum progressively introduces more complex and potentially conflicting scenarios, where the dual-loss objective forces the model to integrate task performance with nuanced human preferences. This staged, multi-objective approach builds a more coherent and capable model than optimizing either objective alone or applying them on mixed-difficulty data.

ideas.title: Adapting Continuum Mechanics and Finite Element Methods for Deformable Object Simulation
ideas.description: This idea challenges the standard assumption of rigid bodies in physical interaction models by proposing an adaptation of methods from continuum mechanics and computational solid mechanics. The analogous field solves the problem of predicting how materials deform and fail by using constitutive laws (e.g., elasticity, plasticity) and numerical techniques like the Finite Element Method (FEM) to discretize and solve the complex governing equations. The adapted research idea is to integrate this framework into robotics and AI simulation engines. This involves defining material parameters for simulated objects, implementing efficient FEM-based or position-based dynamics solvers to handle real-time computation of deformations, and developing new perception and control algorithms that can reason about and manipulate objects that bend, stretch, or break according to these physically grounded models.

ideas.title: Challenge single-LLM as planner assumption

ideas.title: Unified Probabilistic Preference Optimization (UPPO)
ideas.description: This approach introduces a unified probabilistic framework for direct preference optimization from complex feedback. It models the likelihood of any observed preference structure—whether pairwise comparisons, full rankings, or scores on multiple attributes—under a Bradley-Terry or Plackett-Luce style model where the reward is defined by the policy itself. The training objective minimizes the negative log-likelihood of the entire dataset, ensuring statistically consistent updates. This results in a robust, single-stage algorithm that can learn from diverse, real-world preference data without relying on an intermediate reward model or heuristic loss combinations.

ideas.title: Self-Correcting Bayesian Preference Learning with Meta-Learning for Model Assurance
ideas.description: This system combines Bayesian inference for preference learning with a meta-learning framework that continuously audits the primary model's performance and assumptions. It maintains an ensemble of alternative preference models and compares their predictive distributions against incoming user feedback. When evidence of persistent model failure or environmental shift is detected, the system automatically downweights the incumbent model, expands the hypothesis space, or re-allocates exploration to reduce overconfidence. This creates a robust, self-correcting loop where quantified uncertainty reflects true epistemic doubt and the policy avoids catastrophic lock-in to incorrect preferences.

ideas.title: Automated Complexity-Aware Curriculum for DPO via Online Uncertainty Scoring
ideas.description: This approach structures DPO training using a curriculum defined not by static human labels of difficulty, but by the training model's own evolving uncertainty. A scoring module evaluates each candidate preference pair by measuring the model's loss variance or prediction confidence gap between the chosen and rejected responses. Training proceeds on a batch-by-batch basis, selecting data where the model shows moderate but correctable uncertainty, thereby automatically focusing on the most learnable 'frontier' of complex behaviors. This creates a self-paced, adaptive curriculum that robustly progresses toward sophisticated alignment without predefined difficulty tiers.

ideas.title: Reference-Free Alignment via Self-Consistency Regularization
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from statistical mechanics, where systems are analyzed using relative changes and internal consistency laws when an absolute reference state is unavailable. The analogous solution uses conservation principles to relate changes within the system. Adapted to DPO, this leads to a variant that eliminates the need for the original pre-trained reference model. The alignment is achieved by designing a loss function that regularizes the policy model's updates to be self-consistent—for instance, by ensuring the KL divergence between the model's outputs before and after an update on a set of neutral 'anchor' prompts remains bounded, or by deriving a reference distribution directly from the pairwise preference data's statistical properties. This creates a closed-loop alignment process anchored in the model's own behavior and the data's structure, rather than an external static reference.

ideas.title: Stable Online Preference Optimization with Decoupled Reference Policy and Dynamic Regularization
ideas.description: This research develops a stable online algorithm for direct preference optimization (DPO) that overcomes catastrophic forgetting and instability. The core design maintains a slowly updated 'anchor' policy, which provides a stable regularization target, while a separate online policy is updated on a stream of new preference data. The system incorporates a dynamic replay buffer and adjusts regularization strength in real-time based on estimated distribution shift, ensuring robust learning without collapse. This enables safe and efficient iterative alignment of LLMs with continuously evolving human preferences.

ideas.title: Principle-Guided Synthetic Preference Generation for Alignment
ideas.description: This system generates large-scale preference data by using a set of explicit, programmable principles—rather than a pre-trained preference model—to judge pairs of synthetically generated responses. A generator model produces multiple candidate responses to a diverse set of prompts, which are then scored by verifiers checking for adherence to defined principles like factuality, safety, and coherence. The resulting principle-based preference pairs are used to train a DPO policy, ensuring the alignment signal comes from external, controllable criteria instead of a self-reinforcing loop, leading to more robust and scalable improvement.

ideas.title: Adaptive Preference Alignment via Ecological Niche Modeling
ideas.description: This research challenges the static preference dataset assumption by drawing an analogy from ecology, where a species' optimal habitat (its niche) is non-stationary and context-dependent on environmental variables. The analogous solution uses dynamic occupancy models to track and forecast these shifting niches. Adapted to AI alignment, the idea is to model human preferences as a dynamic niche, developing alignment frameworks that employ continuous monitoring, state-space modeling to filter noise from genuine drift, and forecasting mechanisms. This shifts the paradigm from training on a fixed historical dataset to maintaining an adaptive model that anticipates and responds to the evolving landscape of human values and contextual judgments.

ideas.title: Preference Imputation via Collaborative Filtering for Language Models
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a complete set of preference comparisons is available for all prompt types by adapting a solution from Recommender Systems. In that field, collaborative filtering handles extremely sparse user-item rating matrices by imputing missing values based on patterns of similarity, often via matrix factorization. Adapted to language model alignment, we would frame prompts and model responses as analogous to users and items, constructing a sparse matrix from available human preference data. By applying collaborative filtering techniques, we can learn latent embeddings for prompt types and response features, thereby inferring probable human preferences for novel or undersampled prompts. This allows for robust reward model training and policy optimization even when direct preference data is missing for vast regions of the prompt space.

ideas.title: Robust Preference Alignment Under Non-Stationarity via Meta-Preference and Reflective Equilibrium Modeling
ideas.description: This research develops a framework for AI alignment that dynamically tracks human preferences while robustly resisting harmful drift. It does so by learning and applying a model of human meta-preferences—preferences about how and when preferences themselves should change—derived from longitudinal behavior and reflective feedback. The system evaluates observed preference shifts for coherence with deeper values and long-term endorsement, enabling it to adapt to genuine evolution while filtering out noise, malicious influence, or transient context. This results in an alignment approach that is both temporally adaptive and normatively stable.

ideas.title: Adapting Immunological Negative Selection to DPO via Contrastive Learning
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from computational immunology, specifically the 'negative selection' algorithm used in Artificial Immune Systems to distinguish self from non-self. The analogous solution involves training a set of detectors by exposing them to a comprehensive set of 'self' patterns and eliminating any that match, ensuring the remaining system is specialized for detecting novel anomalies. Adapted to Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), this translates to a contrastive learning framework that actively mines or constructs a broad distribution of 'hard negative' responses (non-self) for each prompt—such as plausible but flawed, irrelevant, or unsafe completions. The DPO objective is then augmented with a noise-contrastive or contrastive loss term that explicitly penalizes the policy for generating these hard negatives, while reinforcing the chosen positive response. This creates a more efficient and discriminative learning process, as the model learns a sharper boundary between preferred outputs and a wider universe of incorrect ones, potentially improving sample efficiency and final policy robustness.

ideas.title: Generalized Preference Imputation from Sparse, Complex Feedback
ideas.description: This research direction develops a framework that generalizes Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to learn from incomplete, complex preference signals—such as partial rankings, multi-attribute ratings, or win/loss/tie outcomes—that are observed for only a tiny fraction of possible prompts or response pairs. The core synergy is a model that jointly infers a latent, structured preference representation (e.g., a utility function over response attributes or a ranking model) while actively imputing missing preferences across the prompt space, using the learned structure to guide the imputation. This allows for robust alignment from datasets that are both informationally rich in format but severely limited in coverage, moving beyond the need for complete pairwise comparisons.

ideas.title: Multi-Objective Optimization via Pareto-Efficient Regularization for DPO and SFT
ideas.description: This research idea adapts a solution from Evolutionary Multi-Objective Optimization (EMO) to the challenge of jointly optimizing for human preference alignment (via DPO) and task-specific accuracy (via SFT). In EMO, Pareto optimization methods like ParEGO are used to manage competing objectives by scalarizing them with a tunable preference vector to map the trade-off frontier. Adapted here, we propose a multi-loss setup that employs a Pareto-efficient scalarization (e.g., an augmented Tchebycheff function) of the DPO and SFT losses, rather than a fixed linear combination. This framework allows for the controlled exploration and identification of models on the Pareto front, providing a principled method to balance and scale the dual objectives of alignment and accuracy based on deployable requirements.

ideas.title: Synthetic Preference Data Generation via Directed Evolutionary Sampling
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational chemistry—directed virtual screening for drug discovery—to the problem of scaling preference data. In that field, a scoring function guides the generation of novel, synthetically accessible molecules with desired properties, avoiding exhaustive exploration. Here, the DPO framework's implicit preference model is used as the scoring function to direct the synthetic generation of text pairs. The process involves an iterative loop: proposing candidate response pairs (via a base language model), evaluating their alignment with the DPO objective (i.e., how well they differentiate preferred from dispreferred outputs), and selectively retaining those that provide maximal learning signal. This creates a focused, large-scale synthetic preference dataset, analogous to a focused chemical library, for efficiently training or refining preference-aligned models.

ideas.title: Distributed Preference Optimization via Hierarchical Consensus
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to scale Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) for trillion-parameter models by drawing an analogy from large-scale simulations in Computational Fluid Dynamics. In CFD, simulating continent-scale systems uses domain decomposition and hierarchical consensus protocols (e.g., multigrid methods) to manage memory and compute distribution while maintaining global solution fidelity. The adapted idea applies this principle by developing a memory-efficient, distributed DPO variant. The trillion-parameter model and its associated preference dataset are decomposed across a compute cluster. Each node performs local DPO updates on its assigned model partition and data subset. A novel, lightweight hierarchical consensus protocol, inspired by multigrid solvers, is then designed to efficiently synchronize these distributed updates. This protocol operates at multiple levels of granularity to resolve 'boundary' conflicts between sub-models and iteratively steer all partitions toward a globally optimal policy that aligns with the complete set of human preferences, thereby bypassing the prohibitive memory and communication costs of a monolithic training approach.

ideas.title: Generalized Preference Optimization via Plackett-Luce Models from Psychometrics
ideas.description: This idea proposes to generalize DPO beyond simple pairwise comparisons by drawing an analogy from psychometrics. The analogous field is discrete choice modeling, where the Plackett-Luce model is a standard solution for analyzing ranking data. It generalizes pairwise comparisons by modeling the probability of a full ranking as a product of sequential choice probabilities based on item strengths. The adapted research idea is to integrate the Plackett-Luce model into the DPO framework. This would involve defining a loss function based on the likelihood of observed rankings of multiple model completions for a given prompt, enabling the model to learn from more complex preference structures like ranked lists or multi-attribute feedback aggregated into a single ranking. This approach allows for more data-efficient learning from human feedback that is naturally expressed as rankings.

ideas.title: Inverse Preference Reconstruction via Geophysical Tomography Methods
ideas.description: This research adapts methods from geophysical tomography to solve the inverse problem of extracting preferences from a DPO-trained policy. In seismic tomography, subsurface structures are inferred from surface measurements of seismic waves; here, we treat the policy as the observable 'surface pattern' and aim to reconstruct the 'subsurface' preference dataset that could have produced it. The adapted solution involves developing an iterative inversion algorithm that performs forward simulation (training a policy on a candidate preference dataset) and backward update (adjusting the inferred preferences to minimize divergence from the target policy), regularized by assumptions about human preference structure (e.g., sparsity, consistency). This approach could reveal the implicit preference geometry embedded in RLHF-aligned models and provide tools for auditing and interpreting their ethical foundations.

ideas.title: Analyzing DPO Optimization as a Dynamical System with Lyapunov Methods
ideas.description: This research adapts solution methods from control theory and nonlinear dynamical systems to formally study the convergence properties and loss landscape of the Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) objective. The analogous problem is analyzing the stability of complex nonlinear systems, which is solved using Lyapunov theory—constructing a scalar function that decreases along system trajectories to prove convergence. By treating the DPO gradient flow as a dynamical system, we can adapt this approach: construct a candidate Lyapunov function based on the DPO objective or a related potential. This framework will be used to rigorously prove convergence guarantees (e.g., to the optimal policy or a Nash equilibrium), characterize the stability of fixed points, map basins of attraction, and identify problematic regions in the loss landscape (e.g., where policy collapse or oscillations may occur), providing a more fundamental theoretical understanding of DPO's optimization behavior.

ideas.title: Non-Parametric Preference Modeling via Rank Aggregation
ideas.description: This research idea challenges the standard Bradley-Terry model assumption in RLHF by drawing an analogy to solutions in social choice theory. In voting systems, the problem of aggregating individual ranked ballots into a collective ranking is solved without assuming a specific parametric model for the probability of one candidate beating another. Analogous solutions like the Kemeny-Young or Schulze methods work directly on the observed ordinal comparison data. Adapted to alignment, this suggests developing methods that treat a dataset of human pairwise (or k-wise) preferences as a set of 'votes' between model outputs. The goal is to aggregate these into a global reward ordering or score for training, using non-parametric rank aggregation algorithms. This approach relaxes the strict probabilistic assumptions of Bradley-Terry, potentially leading to more robust preference modeling that can handle violations like non-transitive preferences or heterogeneous rater behavior, without being constrained by a specific functional form for the preference probability.

ideas.title: Adaptive Immune System Sampling for Lightweight DPO
ideas.description: This research adapts computational immunology's model of adaptive immune response to create a lightweight DPO variant for on-device tuning. The analogous solution from immunology involves how biological systems learn from limited pathogen exposure through clonal selection, affinity maturation, and memory cell formation rather than exhaustive data storage. The adapted idea implements a dynamic, immune-inspired sampling strategy for DPO where a small model on an edge device maintains a curated 'memory bank' of the most informative preference pairs. Through selective amplification of challenging contrasts and synthetic generation of informative preference gradients (akin to somatic hypermutation), the system achieves effective fine-tuning with dramatically reduced preference data requirements and computational overhead, enabling practical on-device personalization with limited user interaction data.

ideas.title: Uncertainty-Aware Preference Synthesis with Principle-Based Fallbacks
ideas.description: This approach addresses extreme preference sparsity by synthesizing a robust preference proxy. It combines any available human preferences with automatically generated critiques based on a fixed set of safety, helpfulness, and honesty principles. The model explicitly estimates its uncertainty for any given prompt and uses high-uncertainty regions to trigger constrained, principle-guided generation or deferral mechanisms, rather than ungrounded extrapolation. This ensures controlled, predictable behavior even for entirely novel prompt types where direct preference data is absent.
